target.title: fix(gateway): filter transcript-only history rows
target.body:
Fixes #85669

Summary:
- Filters transcript-only OpenClaw assistant rows from `sessions_history` and session-history snapshot/SSE views.
- Drops `delivery-mirror` rows and only suppresses `gateway-injected` rows when they duplicate the preceding `acp-runtime` text reply.
- Preserves legitimate visible `gateway-injected` assistant replies in history surfaces and live display projection.
- Fetches a wider raw history window before filtering, then preserves the existing default 200-message visible window when callers omit `limit`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `sessions_history` and session-history snapshot/SSE views no longer surface OpenClaw transcript-only `delivery-mirror` rows or duplicate ACP `gateway-injected` mirrors, while preserving legitimate visible `gateway-injected` assistant replies and the existing no-limit visible-history default.

Real environment tested: Local macOS checkout at commit `6883fc4ad225b45fd15a5a3dfd06f260e3b4f249` in `/Users/godcorn/cursor/open-source/projects/openclaw-85669`, using the repository's Node/Vitest runtime and local gateway HTTP e2e coverage for session history.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-core.config.ts src/gateway/session-history-state.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts -t "keeps OpenClaw injected assistant rows"
OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=180000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.e2e.config.ts src/gateway/sessions-history-http.test.ts
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
git diff --check
```

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the after-fix run:
```text
src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts: 21 tests passed
src/gateway/session-history-state.test.ts: 18 tests passed
src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts -t keeps OpenClaw injected assistant rows: 1 passed, 103 skipped
src/gateway/sessions-history-http.test.ts: 16 tests passed
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo: exit code 0
git diff --check: no output
```

Observed result after fix: The filtered history surfaces keep visible content such as `runtime reply`, `visible injected reply`, `visible tool mirror`, and `real answer`; they drop `delivery-mirror` rows and the duplicate `gateway-injected` row that repeats the preceding `acp-runtime` text. The live display regression still keeps OpenClaw injected assistant rows visible. Default `sessions_history` calls still return the latest 200 visible messages after raw overfetch/filtering; the new regression test confirms a 250-message raw response is trimmed to messages 51 through 250.

What was not tested: I did not replay a production Telegram or dashboard session against an installed gateway. The validation is local source-level regression coverage plus the local HTTP session-history e2e path.

target.title: fix(webchat): broadcast agent TTS media final payloads
target.body:
## Summary
- Fixes #81468.
- Bridges WebChat-managed agent-run TTS/media supplements into the final `chat` broadcast payload, so the live WebChat path receives the audio attachment that was already appended to the transcript.
- Keeps media-bearing `block` replies from being saved as the eventual final broadcast snapshot; only actual `final` deliveries can populate that final media message.
- When a final media reply follows an earlier media block, rewrites the same assistant-media transcript entry so live final delivery and `chat.history` reload show the same final audio attachment.
- Adds running Gateway proof that sends `chat.send` over WebSocket, first delivers a media block, then delivers the final TTS media reply, verifies the assistant-media HTTP route can serve the MP3, and confirms `chat.history` has the same final audio without duplicate assistant entries.

## Verification
- `env COREPACK_HOME=/private/tmp/openclaw-corepack node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts -t "agent.*media|auto-TTS final media|media-bearing block" --reporter verbose`
  - `Test Files 2 passed (2)`
  - `Tests 10 passed | 166 skipped (176)`
  - `Duration 6.57s`
- `env COREPACK_HOME=/private/tmp/openclaw-corepack node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts -t "chat.send broadcasts agent-run TTS audio over the running gateway" --reporter verbose`
  - `Test Files 1 passed (1)`
  - `Tests 1 passed | 29 skipped (30)`
  - `Duration 4.89s`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Agent-run auto-TTS/audio replies could be persisted as a WebChat assistant-media transcript supplement while the live final WebChat `chat` event omitted the audio attachment. A media-bearing `block` delivery could also be captured as the final media broadcast candidate and mask a later real final media reply. This patch keeps block-media transcript compatibility, only lets actual final media deliveries populate the live final broadcast snapshot, rewrites the same assistant-media transcript entry when a later final replaces an earlier block, and keeps TTS spoken text out of the visible final/history copy.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS at head `b9c5e58dccb9658084a2058b6a83523ef158f63a`, based on `origin/main` `23e9bc8c0b61f37851136dc5da2311935e348f5d`. I ran the repo's connected Control UI Gateway server test, which starts a real local Gateway on `127.0.0.1`, uses a real local WebSocket client to send `chat.send`, and uses real local HTTP fetches against `http://127.0.0.1:<port>/__openclaw__/assistant-media` for metadata and ticketed media download.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
- `env COREPACK_HOME=/private/tmp/openclaw-corepack node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts -t "chat.send broadcasts agent-run TTS audio over the running gateway" --reporter verbose`
- The mocked agent dispatch starts an agent run in the running Gateway, sends a media-bearing `block` reply from `block.mp3`, then sends the final TTS reply from `tts.mp3`.
- The test waits for the WebSocket final `chat` event, calls the assistant-media metadata route, calls the ticketed media download route, reloads `chat.history`, and verifies history exposes the same final `tts.mp3` attachment rather than the earlier `block.mp3`.

Evidence after fix:
```txt
✓ |gateway-server| ../../src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts > gateway server chat > chat.send broadcasts agent-run TTS audio over the running gateway 384ms

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  1 passed | 29 skipped (30)
Duration  4.89s
```

The focused handler regression also passed after the final patch:
```txt
✓ |gateway-client| ... persists agent-run audio replies emitted as media-bearing block payloads 99ms
✓ |gateway-client| ... persists auto-TTS final media as audio-only so webchat does not duplicate assistant text 25ms
✓ |gateway-client| ... broadcasts final agent media instead of an earlier media-bearing block 22ms
✓ |gateway-methods| ... persists agent-run audio replies emitted as media-bearing block payloads 98ms
✓ |gateway-methods| ... persists auto-TTS final media as audio-only so webchat does not duplicate assistant text 28ms
✓ |gateway-methods| ... broadcasts final agent media instead of an earlier media-bearing block 48ms

Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  10 passed | 166 skipped (176)
Duration  6.57s
```

Observed result after fix: The WebSocket final event arrived with `runId: idem-running-gateway-agent-tts`, `sessionKey: agent:main:main`, and `state: final`. The final assistant content included `{ type: "text", text: "Audio reply" }` plus an audio attachment for the final `tts.mp3` path with `kind: "audio"`, `mimeType: "audio/mpeg"`, and `isVoiceNote: true`. The earlier `block.mp3` payload did not replace the final `tts.mp3` broadcast. The final WebSocket message did not contain the model transcript spoken text. The assistant-media metadata request returned HTTP 200 with `available: true` and a `v1.` media ticket; the ticketed media download returned HTTP 200 and 4 bytes. `chat.history` returned exactly one assistant message for the run, did not contain the spoken TTS text, and contained the same final `tts.mp3` attachment rather than `block.mp3`. The block-only media regression asserted no final broadcast was emitted from a media block by itself.

What was not tested: No real Microsoft/Xiaomi TTS credentials were used; the test uses a local MP3 fixture while exercising the actual Gateway WebSocket final event, assistant-media HTTP route, ticketed media download, transcript rewrite, and history dedupe path. Browser autoplay behavior was not tested. Local full prod/test typecheck was not runnable from this sparse checkout because tracked `packages` and `ui` inputs are not present here; GitHub Actions runs those checks in the full repository checkout.

## AI Assistance Disclosure
This PR was prepared with AI assistance. I reviewed the changed code path and verified the targeted tests above.

target.title: fix(imessage): send group media via attachment command
target.body:
## Summary

Recreates #86720 after #86734 reverted it so the #83365 iMessage group media fix can go through a clean review cycle.

Fixes iMessage media-only sends to explicit chat targets by routing them through `imsg send-attachment --transport auto` instead of the generic RPC `send` payload with a file path.

## Why

While testing #83365 on Lobster, normal iMessage group image replies generated the PNG but did not deliver a media attachment. Direct `imsg send-rich --file` also failed on the same group with an IMCore attachment staging error, while `imsg send-attachment --transport auto` succeeded and produced a real attachment row.

DM media sends by handle already worked, so this keeps handle/DM sends on the existing path and only changes explicit chat media-only sends (`chat_id` / `chat_guid`).

## Changes

- Add a CLI JSON helper for the iMessage send runtime.
- For media-only explicit chat sends, resolve `chat_id` to a chat GUID when needed and call `send-attachment --transport auto`.
- Preserve existing RPC send behavior for DM handle media sends and text/caption sends.
- Add unit coverage for chat GUID, chat ID, and DM handle media behavior.

## Prior work / credit

This intentionally recreates the previously merged #86720 after its procedural revert in #86734. Original fix and Lobster proof by @omarshahine.

## Real behavior proof

Lobster Mac proof from #86720 / #83365:

- Generated media-only iMessage group reply produced the PNG but did not land through the normal send path.
- Direct `imsg send-rich --file` failed for the same group with an IMCore staging error.
- `imsg send-attachment --transport auto` succeeded and produced a visible attachment row in the group chat.

## Verification

- `git diff --cached --check`
- `pnpm format:check -- extensions/imessage/src/send.ts extensions/imessage/src/send.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/imessage/src/send.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/actions.runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`

Fixes #83365

target.title: fix(imessage): dedupe accounts sharing the local Messages source
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #65141. When `channels.imessage.accounts` contained both `default` and a named account pointing at the same local Messages source, the gateway started two `imsg rpc` watchers and inbound messages were processed twice (one `ping` yielded two `pong` replies).

This mirrors the Discord duplicate-token pattern (`extensions/discord/src/accounts.ts:172`) for iMessage:

- `resolveIMessageAccountSourceSignature(account)` derives a `(cliPath, dbPath)` signature for the local backend an account targets.
- `resolveIMessageAccountSourceOwner({ cfg, signature })` picks the canonical owner, **preferring an explicit named account over the implicit `default`** so bindings tied to a named account keep delivering.
- `isIMessageAccountEnabledForRuntime` / `resolveIMessageAccountDisabledReason` are wired into the iMessage channel config adapter, so the existing Gateway `startAccount` loop (`src/gateway/server-channels.ts:458-473`) skips non-owner duplicates and reports `duplicate iMessage source; using account "<owner>"` in runtime state.
- `imessageDoctor.collectPreviewWarnings` flags the redundant account from `openclaw doctor` with source coordinates and the `enabled: false` silencing tip. The doctor adapter moved from an inline `{ groupAllowFromFallbackToAllowFrom: false }` into a proper `extensions/imessage/src/doctor.ts` to host the new warning.

User config in the issue (`default` + `swang430-gmail-com`) now starts one `imsg rpc` and the named account keeps receiving the binding, matching the workaround the reporter applied manually.

## Verification

Behavior addressed: duplicate `imsg rpc` watchers and duplicate inbound replies when two enabled iMessage accounts share the local backend.

Real environment tested: synthetic via the new vitest cases; not a live iMessage replay.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/imessage
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/imessage/src/accounts.ts extensions/imessage/src/accounts.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/channel.ts extensions/imessage/src/doctor.ts extensions/imessage/src/doctor.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/shared.ts
pnpm format:check extensions/imessage/src/accounts.ts extensions/imessage/src/doctor.ts extensions/imessage/src/channel.ts extensions/imessage/src/shared.ts extensions/imessage/src/accounts.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/doctor.test.ts
node scripts/check-changelog-attributions.mjs
```

Evidence after fix: `Test Files 47 passed (47) / Tests 452 passed (452)` across the iMessage extension suite (includes the new collision cases in `accounts.test.ts` and `doctor.test.ts`). Typecheck, lint, formatting, and changelog attribution scans all exit clean.

Observed result after fix: `listEnabledIMessageAccounts(cfg)` returns one account per unique `(cliPath, dbPath)`, the non-owner reports `duplicate iMessage source; using account "<owner>"`, and `imessageDoctor.collectPreviewWarnings` emits one `- channels.imessage: accounts "<owner>" and "<dup>" watch the same local Messages source (cliPath=imsg)...` line per collision group; no warning when only one account is enabled or when each account targets a distinct cliPath.

What was not tested: a live Mac with two configured iMessage accounts running `imsg rpc` end-to-end. Not local Crabbox-validated; broader changed lanes are deferred to CI.

## Notes

- No SDK contract or config schema change. Existing iMessage configs continue to load without migration.
- The owner-preference rule keeps explicit account ids working when paired with `default`. Reverse case (two named accounts targeting the same backend) still dedupes deterministically via `listIMessageAccountIds` order.
- CHANGELOG entry placed under `## Unreleased` § Fixes, thanking @swang430 who reported the original issue with a complete repro.

target.title: fix(ui): keep audio and document inline data URLs out of optimistic chat state
target.body:
## Summary

- Extended the `isInlineDataUrl` guard in `sendChatMessage` to cover **all** attachment MIME types (image, audio, document), not just images
- When a `previewUrl` falls back to an inline `data:` URL for audio (.m4a, .mp3, .wav, .ogg) or document (.pdf) attachments, the large base64 payload is now replaced with a short text placeholder (e.g. `Attached audio: recording.m4a`) instead of being embedded in Lit state where it triggers `RangeError: Maximum call stack size exceeded`
- Renamed `formatInlineImageAttachmentPlaceholder` to `formatInlineAttachmentPlaceholder` to handle all attachment types generically

## Fixes

Fixes #86738 — scope gap from #73360 / #82228 which only guarded image attachments against inline data URL embedding

## Verification

- All 63 tests in `ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts` pass
- All 45 tests in `ui/src/ui/app-chat.test.ts` pass
- Added dedicated tests for audio and document inline data URL handling

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Observed result after fix: The filtered history surfaces keep visible content such as `runtime reply`, `visible injected reply`, `visible tool mirror`, and `real answer`; they drop `delivery-mirror` rows and the duplicate `gateway-injected` row that repeats the preceding `acp-runtime` text. The live display regression still keeps OpenClaw injected assistant rows visible. Default `sessions_history` calls still return the latest 200 visible messages after raw overfetch/filtering; the new regression test confirms a 250-message raw response is trimmed to messages 51 through 250.

What was not tested: I did not replay a production Telegram or dashboard session against an installed gateway. The validation is local source-level regression coverage plus the local HTTP session-history e2e path.

target.title: fix(imessage): send group media via attachment command
target.body:
## Summary

Recreates #86720 after #86734 reverted it so the #83365 iMessage group media fix can go through a clean review cycle.

Fixes iMessage media-only sends to explicit chat targets by routing them through `imsg send-attachment --transport auto` instead of the generic RPC `send` payload with a file path.

## Why

While testing #83365 on Lobster, normal iMessage group image replies generated the PNG but did not deliver a media attachment. Direct `imsg send-rich --file` also failed on the same group with an IMCore attachment staging error, while `imsg send-attachment --transport auto` succeeded and produced a real attachment row.

DM media sends by handle already worked, so this keeps handle/DM sends on the existing path and only changes explicit chat media-only sends (`chat_id` / `chat_guid`).

## Changes

- Add a CLI JSON helper for the iMessage send runtime.
- For media-only explicit chat sends, resolve `chat_id` to a chat GUID when needed and call `send-attachment --transport auto`.
- Preserve existing RPC send behavior for DM handle media sends and text/caption sends.
- Add unit coverage for chat GUID, chat ID, and DM handle media behavior.

## Prior work / credit

This intentionally recreates the previously merged #86720 after its procedural revert in #86734. Original fix and Lobster proof by @omarshahine.

## Real behavior proof

Lobster Mac proof from #86720 / #83365:

- Generated media-only iMessage group reply produced the PNG but did not land through the normal send path.
- Direct `imsg send-rich --file` failed for the same group with an IMCore staging error.
- `imsg send-attachment --transport auto` succeeded and produced a visible attachment row in the group chat.

## Verification

- `git diff --cached --check`
- `pnpm format:check -- extensions/imessage/src/send.ts extensions/imessage/src/send.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/imessage/src/send.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/actions.runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`

Fixes #83365

target.title: feat(browser): expand Chrome MCP web capabilities
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the Browser plugin's Chrome MCP existing-session transport only exposed a small subset of the browser automation and debug surface that was available in the local testbed, so real browser workflows still fell back to partial behavior.
- Solution: route the Chrome MCP transport through the richer Browser tool surface, including console/network diagnostics, text and coordinate interactions, dialogs, emulation, tracing, heap snapshots, Lighthouse, screencasts, extension inventory, and safer existing-session cleanup.
- What changed: updates are scoped to Browser plugin runtime/tests under `extensions/browser/...`; focused coverage was added for Chrome MCP routing, existing-session behavior, wrapper forwarding, live-surface behavior, CDP helpers, and profile cleanup ownership.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not modify live OpenClaw config, Gateway service config, credentials, Microsoft Teams/M365 tenant configuration, personal Chrome profiles, or `CHANGELOG.md`.

AI-assisted: yes. I understand the Browser plugin changes in this PR and ran the real behavior proof and local validation described below.

## Motivation

- This makes the Browser plugin's Chrome MCP existing-session mode useful for full web workflows instead of only basic navigation/click/readback.
- It also tightens cleanup behavior so dedicated OpenClaw-managed profiles can be stopped safely without targeting arbitrary personal or custom Chrome profiles.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related N/A
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: Chrome MCP-backed Browser sessions now expose and execute the broader live web capability surface from a real OpenClaw setup, while preserving dedicated-profile cleanup boundaries.

Real environment tested: Linux host, Node/pnpm source checkout, OpenClaw Browser plugin with a dedicated `agent-chrome` profile, Chrome MCP existing-session transport, local loopback test fixture, and redacted/non-public runtime details.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
git diff --check
timeout 300s corepack pnpm exec vitest run extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.live.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.stop-running-browser.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.existing-session.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/routes/agent.existing-session.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/cdp.helpers.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/cdp.screenshot-params.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/config.test.ts
timeout 240s corepack pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/browser/tsconfig.json --noEmit
corepack pnpm check:changed
```

Evidence after fix: copied live output/readback from the same host after validation:

```text
git diff --check: pass
focused Browser vitest: 7 passed (7); 196 passed | 1 skipped (197)
Browser TypeScript check: pass
check:changed: pass

live suite: 55 pass/evidence lines plus 3 expected guardrails
status/profile/listing: pass
tabs/navigation/snapshot/click/hover/fill/select/key/resize/dialog/upload: pass
fetch + networkidle: pass
drag/drop/evaluate/console/network inspection: pass
screenshot/emulation/trace/heap snapshot/Lighthouse/screencast: pass
extension list/install/reload/tab-id/uninstall: pass
third-party and WebMCP discovery: pass
tab close/browser stop/cleanup: pass

post-validation readback:
Gateway active/running; NRestarts=0
agent-chrome status: enabled=true, driver=existing-session, transport=chrome-mcp, running=false, pid=null
host-owned agent-chrome/chrome-devtools-mcp/watchdog leftovers: none
```

Observed result after fix: the Browser plugin exercised the expanded Chrome MCP web capabilities against a real local setup, generated non-empty runtime artifacts for screenshot/trace/heap/Lighthouse/screencast flows, cleaned up the dedicated Browser profile, and left the Gateway running without a restart.

What was not tested: public CI had not run at PR creation time; cross-OS behavior was not tested locally; a pipe-backed existing-session PDF path still needs reachable HTTP CDP and was treated as an expected guardrail; local `web_fetch` of private loopback URLs remained blocked by SSRF/private-network policy as expected; no Microsoft Teams/M365 tenant or credential behavior was exercised.

Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the pre-patch Chrome MCP path only covered a narrower Browser surface; the after-fix proof above demonstrates the expanded surface end to end.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Chrome MCP support existed, but Browser tool routing and existing-session handling had not been expanded to the full tested browser capability surface.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no focused coverage proving the Browser tool roster and Chrome MCP wrappers could execute the richer live surface.
- Contributing context (if known): several flows need CDP or Chrome MCP-specific routing instead of generic Browser fallbacks.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.live.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.existing-session.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.stop-running-browser.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/routes/agent.existing-session.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/cdp.helpers.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/cdp.screenshot-params.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/config.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: Chrome MCP Browser routing, existing-session startup/readback, cleanup ownership, CDP helper behavior, and live-capability wrapper forwarding.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the focused Browser tests exercise the changed plugin surface without running unrelated package suites.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A for the expanded surface; existing Browser tests covered narrower behavior.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A; focused tests were added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Browser sessions using Chrome MCP existing-session transport can use more Browser tool capabilities for real web workflows.
- Dedicated Browser profile cleanup is stricter about ownership and should avoid stopping arbitrary personal/custom Chrome profiles.
- Unsupported/guarded cases remain explicit instead of silently pretending to work.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[Browser tool request] -> [Chrome MCP existing-session path] -> [partial surface / fallback gaps]

After:
[Browser tool request] -> [Chrome MCP existing-session path] -> [routed Browser capability] -> [real browser result + focused proof]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: the Browser plugin exposes additional browser automation/debug capabilities when the Browser tool is enabled. Mitigations in this PR include dedicated-profile cleanup ownership checks, explicit unsupported-state handling, focused tests around existing-session ownership, and preserving the existing Browser/Gateway permission model. The live proof used a dedicated Browser profile and redacted local runtime details.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node/pnpm source checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Browser plugin, Chrome MCP existing-session transport
- Relevant config (redacted): dedicated `agent-chrome` Browser profile, no personal profile or credential mutation

### Steps

1. Run the focused Browser validation commands listed in Real behavior proof.
2. Start a dedicated Browser profile using the Chrome MCP existing-session transport.
3. Exercise tabs, navigation, interactions, diagnostics, artifacts, extension inventory, and cleanup against a local loopback fixture.
4. Read back Browser status and host process cleanup.

### Expected

- Chrome MCP-backed Browser sessions execute the expanded web capability surface.
- Unsupported guardrails stay explicit.
- Dedicated-profile cleanup leaves no owned Chrome MCP/process leftovers.
- Gateway remains running.

### Actual

- Focused tests, Browser TypeScript check, `git diff --check`, and `check:changed` passed.
- The live host suite recorded 55 pass/evidence lines and 3 expected guardrails.
- Post-validation readback showed Gateway active/running with `NRestarts=0`, Browser profile stopped, and no host-owned leftovers.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Evidence is summarized in copied output above. Local live artifacts included screenshot, Chrome trace, heap snapshot, Lighthouse JSON/HTML, and a non-empty WebM screencast; they are not committed to the repository.

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: focused Browser tests, Browser TypeScript check, changed-file gate, live Browser capability gauntlet, Browser status readback, and cleanup readback.
- Edge cases checked: unsupported PDF path without reachable HTTP CDP, SSRF/private-network guardrail for local `web_fetch`, unavailable third-party/WebMCP executable page tools, dialogs, upload, drag/drop, extension inventory, and cleanup ownership.
- What I did **not** verify: cross-OS behavior, public CI before PR creation, Microsoft Teams/M365 tenant behavior, credential/auth behavior, and local `codex review --base origin/main` because the standalone Codex CLI is intentionally unavailable on this host.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

No bot review conversations existed at PR creation time.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: expanded Browser capability surface may expose more powerful browser automation when Browser is enabled.
  - Mitigation: preserve existing Browser permission/config boundaries and add focused tests for routed capabilities.
- Risk: cleanup code could accidentally target the wrong Chrome profile.
  - Mitigation: cleanup is gated around OpenClaw-managed dedicated profile ownership and covered by focused tests plus live readback.
- Risk: Chrome MCP/CDP capabilities can differ by environment.
  - Mitigation: unsupported states remain explicit, and the PR includes live proof plus focused unit/seam coverage.

target.title: fix(webchat): broadcast agent TTS media final payloads
target.body:
## Summary
- Fixes #81468.
- Bridges WebChat-managed agent-run TTS/media supplements into the final `chat` broadcast payload, so the live WebChat path receives the audio attachment that was already appended to the transcript.
- Keeps media-bearing `block` replies from being saved as the eventual final broadcast snapshot; only actual `final` deliveries can populate that final media message.
- When a final media reply follows an earlier media block, rewrites the same assistant-media transcript entry so live final delivery and `chat.history` reload show the same final audio attachment.
- Adds running Gateway proof that sends `chat.send` over WebSocket, first delivers a media block, then delivers the final TTS media reply, verifies the assistant-media HTTP route can serve the MP3, and confirms `chat.history` has the same final audio without duplicate assistant entries.

## Verification
- `env COREPACK_HOME=/private/tmp/openclaw-corepack node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts -t "agent.*media|auto-TTS final media|media-bearing block" --reporter verbose`
  - `Test Files 2 passed (2)`
  - `Tests 10 passed | 166 skipped (176)`
  - `Duration 6.57s`
- `env COREPACK_HOME=/private/tmp/openclaw-corepack node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts -t "chat.send broadcasts agent-run TTS audio over the running gateway" --reporter verbose`
  - `Test Files 1 passed (1)`
  - `Tests 1 passed | 29 skipped (30)`
  - `Duration 4.89s`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Agent-run auto-TTS/audio replies could be persisted as a WebChat assistant-media transcript supplement while the live final WebChat `chat` event omitted the audio attachment. A media-bearing `block` delivery could also be captured as the final media broadcast candidate and mask a later real final media reply. This patch keeps block-media transcript compatibility, only lets actual final media deliveries populate the live final broadcast snapshot, rewrites the same assistant-media transcript entry when a later final replaces an earlier block, and keeps TTS spoken text out of the visible final/history copy.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS at head `b9c5e58dccb9658084a2058b6a83523ef158f63a`, based on `origin/main` `23e9bc8c0b61f37851136dc5da2311935e348f5d`. I ran the repo's connected Control UI Gateway server test, which starts a real local Gateway on `127.0.0.1`, uses a real local WebSocket client to send `chat.send`, and uses real local HTTP fetches against `http://127.0.0.1:<port>/__openclaw__/assistant-media` for metadata and ticketed media download.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
- `env COREPACK_HOME=/private/tmp/openclaw-corepack node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts -t "chat.send broadcasts agent-run TTS audio over the running gateway" --reporter verbose`
- The mocked agent dispatch starts an agent run in the running Gateway, sends a media-bearing `block` reply from `block.mp3`, then sends the final TTS reply from `tts.mp3`.
- The test waits for the WebSocket final `chat` event, calls the assistant-media metadata route, calls the ticketed media download route, reloads `chat.history`, and verifies history exposes the same final `tts.mp3` attachment rather than the earlier `block.mp3`.

Evidence after fix:
```txt
✓ |gateway-server| ../../src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts > gateway server chat > chat.send broadcasts agent-run TTS audio over the running gateway 384ms

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  1 passed | 29 skipped (30)
Duration  4.89s
```

The focused handler regression also passed after the final patch:
```txt
✓ |gateway-client| ... persists agent-run audio replies emitted as media-bearing block payloads 99ms
✓ |gateway-client| ... persists auto-TTS final media as audio-only so webchat does not duplicate assistant text 25ms
✓ |gateway-client| ... broadcasts final agent media instead of an earlier media-bearing block 22ms
✓ |gateway-methods| ... persists agent-run audio replies emitted as media-bearing block payloads 98ms
✓ |gateway-methods| ... persists auto-TTS final media as audio-only so webchat does not duplicate assistant text 28ms
✓ |gateway-methods| ... broadcasts final agent media instead of an earlier media-bearing block 48ms

Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  10 passed | 166 skipped (176)
Duration  6.57s
```

Observed result after fix: The WebSocket final event arrived with `runId: idem-running-gateway-agent-tts`, `sessionKey: agent:main:main`, and `state: final`. The final assistant content included `{ type: "text", text: "Audio reply" }` plus an audio attachment for the final `tts.mp3` path with `kind: "audio"`, `mimeType: "audio/mpeg"`, and `isVoiceNote: true`. The earlier `block.mp3` payload did not replace the final `tts.mp3` broadcast. The final WebSocket message did not contain the model transcript spoken text. The assistant-media metadata request returned HTTP 200 with `available: true` and a `v1.` media ticket; the ticketed media download returned HTTP 200 and 4 bytes. `chat.history` returned exactly one assistant message for the run, did not contain the spoken TTS text, and contained the same final `tts.mp3` attachment rather than `block.mp3`. The block-only media regression asserted no final broadcast was emitted from a media block by itself.

What was not tested: No real Microsoft/Xiaomi TTS credentials were used; the test uses a local MP3 fixture while exercising the actual Gateway WebSocket final event, assistant-media HTTP route, ticketed media download, transcript rewrite, and history dedupe path. Browser autoplay behavior was not tested. Local full prod/test typecheck was not runnable from this sparse checkout because tracked `packages` and `ui` inputs are not present here; GitHub Actions runs those checks in the full repository checkout.

## AI Assistance Disclosure
This PR was prepared with AI assistance. I reviewed the changed code path and verified the targeted tests above.

target.title: fix(commands): deliver /new and /reset acknowledgements despite source reply suppression
target.body:
## Summary

- The `/new` and `/reset` commands produce acknowledgement replies (✅ Session reset. / ✅ New session started.) that were not delivered to users on channels like Mattermost where `visibleReplies` is set to `message_tool`.
- The acknowledgement reply at `commands-reset.ts:176-178` is now wrapped with `markReplyPayloadForSourceSuppressionDelivery()` so it bypasses source reply suppression, following the same pattern used by other internal notices that must bypass source delivery suppression.

## Verification

- All 1918 tests in the auto-reply-reply test suite pass (114 test files).
- The fix uses the existing `markReplyPayloadForSourceSuppressionDelivery` helper already used by other replies that must bypass source suppression (e.g. ACP reset acknowledgment at line 148).

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** `/new` and `/reset` commands on Mattermost group channels silently change session state without showing visible acknowledgement.

**Environment tested:** Node 22.x on Linux, pnpm test.

**Steps run after the patch:** Verified the production `markReplyPayloadForSourceSuppressionDelivery` function sets the `deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression` metadata flag that the dispatch pipeline checks:

```bash
node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "
import { markReplyPayloadForSourceSuppressionDelivery, getReplyPayloadMetadata } from './src/auto-reply/reply-payload.js';

const reply = markReplyPayloadForSourceSuppressionDelivery({
  text: '✅ Session reset.',
});
const meta = getReplyPayloadMetadata(reply);
console.log('Input reply:', JSON.stringify(reply));
console.log('deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression:', meta?.deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression);
"
```

Output:

```
Input reply: {"text":"✅ Session reset."}
deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression: true
```

**Evidence after fix:**

The fix in `commands-reset.ts` now wraps the reply with `markReplyPayloadForSourceSuppressionDelivery()`:

```typescript
reply: markReplyPayloadForSourceSuppressionDelivery({
  text: commandAction === "reset" ? "✅ Session reset." : "✅ New session started.",
}),
```

The dispatch pipeline in `dispatch-from-config.ts` (line 2414) checks `shouldDeliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression(reply)` before suppressing delivery. The function returns `true` when `deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression === true` and the reply is not already suppressed by send policy, allowing the acknowledgement to reach the user.

**Observed result:** The acknowledgement reply payload carries `deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression: true` metadata. The dispatch check at `dispatch-from-config.ts:2414` allows delivery despite `message_tool_only` source reply suppression.

**Not tested:** Live Mattermost gateway session with `visibleReplies: message_tool` configuration.

Closes #86664.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(imessage): send group media via attachment command
target.body:
## Summary

Recreates #86720 after #86734 reverted it so the #83365 iMessage group media fix can go through a clean review cycle.

Fixes iMessage media-only sends to explicit chat targets by routing them through `imsg send-attachment --transport auto` instead of the generic RPC `send` payload with a file path.

## Why

While testing #83365 on Lobster, normal iMessage group image replies generated the PNG but did not deliver a media attachment. Direct `imsg send-rich --file` also failed on the same group with an IMCore attachment staging error, while `imsg send-attachment --transport auto` succeeded and produced a real attachment row.

DM media sends by handle already worked, so this keeps handle/DM sends on the existing path and only changes explicit chat media-only sends (`chat_id` / `chat_guid`).

## Changes

- Add a CLI JSON helper for the iMessage send runtime.
- For media-only explicit chat sends, resolve `chat_id` to a chat GUID when needed and call `send-attachment --transport auto`.
- Preserve existing RPC send behavior for DM handle media sends and text/caption sends.
- Add unit coverage for chat GUID, chat ID, and DM handle media behavior.

## Prior work / credit

This intentionally recreates the previously merged #86720 after its procedural revert in #86734. Original fix and Lobster proof by @omarshahine.

## Real behavior proof

Lobster Mac proof from #86720 / #83365:

- Generated media-only iMessage group reply produced the PNG but did not land through the normal send path.
- Direct `imsg send-rich --file` failed for the same group with an IMCore staging error.
- `imsg send-attachment --transport auto` succeeded and produced a visible attachment row in the group chat.

## Verification

- `git diff --cached --check`
- `pnpm format:check -- extensions/imessage/src/send.ts extensions/imessage/src/send.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/imessage/src/send.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/actions.runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`

Fixes #83365

target.title: feat:Add Xiaomi Token Plan provider support
target.body:
## Summary

- add first-class `xiaomi-token-plan` support to the bundled Xiaomi provider plugin
- add regional Token Plan onboarding/auth choices (China/Europe/Singapore), static v2.5 catalog entries, and `tp-...` / `sk-...` key-shape validation
- wire the new provider through usage/auth/config overlay surfaces and update Xiaomi provider docs
- set model costs with tiered cache-read pricing: flat input/output per 1M tokens, cache-read pricing steps at 256K input tokens
- use model catalog names as display aliases instead of generic provider labels

## Why

OpenClaw's bundled Xiaomi plugin only supported pay-as-you-go `xiaomi` setup, while issue #86169 asked for Xiaomi MiMo Token Plan support without requiring manual custom-provider wiring.

## Screenshots

![Model dropdown showing specific model names instead of generic provider label](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/NianJiuZst/openclaw/temp-screenshots/screenshot-model-dropdown-fixed.png)

![Gateway dashboard connected with xiaomi-token-plan configured](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/NianJiuZst/openclaw/temp-screenshots/screenshot-dashboard-connected.png)

![Gateway Control UI connected](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/NianJiuZst/openclaw/temp-screenshots/screenshot-gateway-connected.png)

![Gateway dashboard overview](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/NianJiuZst/openclaw/temp-screenshots/screenshot-gateway-dashboard.png)

## Model costs

| Model | Input | Output | cacheRead (<256K) | cacheRead (>=256K) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `mimo-v2.5-pro` | $1/1M | $3/1M | $0.20/1M | $0.40/1M |
| `mimo-v2.5` | $0.4/1M | $2/1M | $0.08/1M | $0.16/1M |

Prices are USD per million tokens. Cache-read pricing uses `tieredPricing` with a step at 256,000 input tokens.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: OpenClaw can onboard and resolve Xiaomi MiMo Token Plan as a first-class provider with regional preset endpoints.

Real environment tested: macOS local source checkout at `b04e67b2` with live Xiaomi Token Plan China (`cn`) region `tp-...` API key against `https://token-plan-cn.xiaomimimo.com/v1`.

Steps:
1. `curl -s "https://token-plan-cn.xiaomimimo.com/v1/models" -H "Authorization: Bearer tp-***"` — HTTP 200, returned 8 models including mimo-v2.5 and mimo-v2.5-pro.
2. `curl -s "https://token-plan-cn.xiaomimimo.com/v1/chat/completions"` with `mimo-v2.5-pro` — HTTP 200, `model: mimo-v2.5-pro`, `usage: prompt=255, completion=20, cacheRead=192`.
3. `curl -s "https://token-plan-cn.xiaomimimo.com/v1/chat/completions"` with `mimo-v2.5` — HTTP 200, valid response.
4. `openclaw agent --local --agent main --model "xiaomi-token-plan/mimo-v2.5" --message "Reply with ok" --thinking off --json` — `text: ok`, `provider: xiaomi-token-plan`, `model: mimo-v2.5`, `fallback: False`, `usage: input=90, output=2, cacheRead=22656`.
5. `openclaw onboard --non-interactive --accept-risk --auth-choice xiaomi-token-plan-cn --xiaomi-token-plan-api-key "tp-***" --json` — config written with correct region baseUrl, model aliases, and auth profile stored.
6. 7 test files, 165 tests passed. `pnpm tsgo` clean. `pnpm build` clean.

Evidence after fix: Provider resolves correctly through auth profile store. Both `mimo-v2.5` and `mimo-v2.5-pro` return valid chat completions with working prompt caching (cacheRead observed in usage). Model dropdown shows specific model names ("Xiaomi MiMo V2.5 Pro"). Full non-interactive `openclaw onboard --auth-choice xiaomi-token-plan-cn` succeeds end-to-end. Costs flow from manifest to usage tracking including tiered cache-read pricing.

What was not tested: The `ams` (Europe) and `sgp` (Singapore) regional endpoints, GitHub Actions / Crabbox / Testbox CI runs.

Fixes #86169.

target.title: [codex] Fix memory close sync race
target.body:
Refs #86702

## Summary

- Register `MemoryIndexManager.sync()` as in-flight before awaiting provider initialization so `close()` can see syncs that are blocked inside provider setup.
- Delay provider and SQLite teardown until the current sync has settled, while still tracking provider references seen during shutdown so fallback swaps can be closed safely.
- Update memory-core regression coverage for both existing pending-sync shutdown and the provider-initialization interleaving.

## Root Cause

`close()` captured `this.syncing` before awaiting provider initialization and watcher teardown. A sync that was already started but still awaiting `ensureProviderInitialized()` could attach `this.syncing` after that stale snapshot, letting `close()` proceed to close provider and database resources while the sync continued.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: `MemoryIndexManager.close()` no longer closes embedding providers or the SQLite database before already-started sync work settles, including syncs blocked in provider initialization.

Real environment tested: Local sparse checkout on macOS arm64 with Node v24.13.1 and installed workspace dependencies. The proof runs a standalone Node runtime script against the real `MemoryIndexManager`, real SQLite index, and the public memory embedding provider registry. The script creates a temporary workspace and memory file, starts `sync({ force: true })`, blocks provider creation, calls `close()`, then releases provider init and holds the real `embedBatch()` call before allowing sync to finish.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
NODE_NO_WARNINGS=1 node --import tsx /private/tmp/openclaw-86765-proof.mjs
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
openclaw#86765 runtime proof
node: v24.13.1
platform: darwin arm64
workspace: /var/folders/qm/d7_ghh9j1019scbft187ry000000gn/T/openclaw-86765-proof-511y6J
scenario: sync blocked during provider init, close() called, provider init released, sync held inside real embedBatch
during blocked sync:
  close settled: false
  provider close calls: 0
  embedBatch calls: 1
  sqlite SELECT 1: ok
after releasing sync:
  close error: none
  provider close calls: 1
  sqlite after close: database is not open
events: provider:create:entered -> provider:create:resolved -> provider:embedBatch:start:1 -> provider:embedBatch:released -> provider:close:1
PASS: close waited for the in-flight sync before closing provider/database resources
```

Observed result after fix: While sync was blocked inside the real `embedBatch()` path, `close()` had not settled, the embedding provider had not been closed, and SQLite still answered `SELECT 1`. After releasing sync, `close()` completed without error, the provider closed exactly once, and SQLite reported `database is not open`, which shows resource teardown happened after the in-flight sync settled.

What was not tested: The proof uses a local registered provider adapter to make the race deterministic; it does not call external embedding APIs. Full repository lint/check was not run locally. The repo `run-oxlint.mjs` wrapper tries to prepare extension package boundary artifacts for unrelated sparse-checkout extensions, so the PR uses direct touched-file `oxfmt`/`oxlint` plus the relevant memory tests.

## Verification

- `NODE_NO_WARNINGS=1 node --import tsx /private/tmp/openclaw-86765-proof.mjs` - passed, runtime proof above
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts --configLoader runner extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.async-search.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.readonly-recovery.test.ts` - passed, 3 files / 32 tests
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts` - passed
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts` - passed
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD` - passed

target.title: perf(agents): cache provider tool schema normalization
target.body:
## Summary

- Cache provider-normalized tool schema parameters for repeated embedded agent turns when the active provider hook exposes a stable normalization-hook identity.
- Keep the cache conservative and hook-owned: key includes the provider hook identity plus provider/model/API/base URL and ordered tool names with stable original schema signatures; values store only cloned `parameters`, never tool objects, execute closures, approval state, or guards.
- Add `OPENCLAW_TOOL_SCHEMA_CACHE=0` as a kill switch, with cache-key, clone-safety, env-disable, and eviction coverage.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** repeated embedded agent turns no longer redo provider tool-schema normalization when the provider hook identity, provider/model/config inputs, and ordered tool schema signature are unchanged. Fresh tool objects, `execute` closures, approval state, and runtime guards are still rebuilt per turn.
- **Real environment tested:** local OpenClaw checkout at `/Users/guanbear/workspace/openclaw-5.4-src`, branch `tool-schema-cache-pr3`, head `a5874d8ac60cf2974b74cc66b50028db103b390e`, macOS host, Node 25.9.0 via repo `pnpm` tooling.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ran `pnpm exec tsx --input-type=module` locally with a script that imports `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/tool-schema-runtime.ts`, uses the real bundled OpenAI provider hook path, recreates 50 representative OpenAI-style tool objects and fresh `execute` closures per iteration, then runs 100 normalization iterations with `OPENCLAW_TOOL_SCHEMA_CACHE=0` and 100 iterations with the default cache enabled. Also ran a same-Gateway-process mock OpenAI E2E before the rebase with five main-agent RPC calls per mode and 27 tools sent per model request.
- **Evidence after fix:** current-head terminal output from the provider schema-normalization microbenchmark:

```json
{
  "head": "a5874d8ac60cf2974b74cc66b50028db103b390e",
  "cases": [
    {
      "label": "cache-disabled",
      "iterations": 100,
      "toolCount": 50,
      "ms": 63625,
      "perIterationMs": 636.25,
      "rssDeltaMb": 447.7,
      "stats": {
        "bypass": 100,
        "hit": 0,
        "miss": 0,
        "store": 0,
        "size": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "label": "cache-enabled",
      "iterations": 100,
      "toolCount": 50,
      "ms": 26927,
      "perIterationMs": 269.27,
      "rssDeltaMb": -3.1,
      "stats": {
        "bypass": 0,
        "hit": 99,
        "miss": 1,
        "store": 1,
        "size": 1
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Additional lightweight mock Gateway E2E evidence from the same PR code before the rebase:

- cache enabled durations `[2105, 496, 471, 465, 470]`, p50 `471ms`, 5 model requests, 27 tools per request
- cache disabled durations `[2002, 482, 472, 474, 470]`, p50 `474ms`, 5 model requests, 27 tools per request

- **Observed result after fix:** the provider schema-normalization cache is active on the real bundled OpenAI provider hook path: the benchmark stores once, then records 99 cache hits while recreating tool objects and closures every iteration. The current conservative implementation reduces the repeated normalization path from `636.25ms` to `269.27ms` per iteration in this 50-tool fixture and cuts RSS growth, but the hit path still pays cache-key and original-schema-signature construction cost. The mock Gateway E2E confirms the real main-agent path still succeeds with the cache enabled, but it does not show a meaningful wall-latency delta in a five-run mocked fixture.
- **What was not tested:** full live-provider end-to-end agent reply latency was not tested. This PR caches only provider-normalized schema `parameters`; it does not cache full tool inventory/materialization, prompt-report inputs, `core-plugin-tools`, policy resolution, or complete agent-turn startup. The microbenchmark should not be read as a 400ms-per-turn full agent latency claim.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/tool-schema-runtime.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/tools.test.ts`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- Current-head scoped `pnpm exec tsx` provider schema-normalization microbenchmark described above
- `git diff --check`

target.title: fix(webchat): broadcast agent TTS media final payloads
target.body:
## Summary
- Fixes #81468.
- Bridges WebChat-managed agent-run TTS/media supplements into the final `chat` broadcast payload, so the live WebChat path receives the audio attachment that was already appended to the transcript.
- Keeps media-bearing `block` replies from being saved as the eventual final broadcast snapshot; only actual `final` deliveries can populate that final media message.
- When a final media reply follows an earlier media block, rewrites the same assistant-media transcript entry so live final delivery and `chat.history` reload show the same final audio attachment.
- Adds running Gateway proof that sends `chat.send` over WebSocket, first delivers a media block, then delivers the final TTS media reply, verifies the assistant-media HTTP route can serve the MP3, and confirms `chat.history` has the same final audio without duplicate assistant entries.

## Verification
- `env COREPACK_HOME=/private/tmp/openclaw-corepack node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts -t "agent.*media|auto-TTS final media|media-bearing block" --reporter verbose`
  - `Test Files 2 passed (2)`
  - `Tests 10 passed | 166 skipped (176)`
  - `Duration 6.57s`
- `env COREPACK_HOME=/private/tmp/openclaw-corepack node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts -t "chat.send broadcasts agent-run TTS audio over the running gateway" --reporter verbose`
  - `Test Files 1 passed (1)`
  - `Tests 1 passed | 29 skipped (30)`
  - `Duration 4.89s`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Agent-run auto-TTS/audio replies could be persisted as a WebChat assistant-media transcript supplement while the live final WebChat `chat` event omitted the audio attachment. A media-bearing `block` delivery could also be captured as the final media broadcast candidate and mask a later real final media reply. This patch keeps block-media transcript compatibility, only lets actual final media deliveries populate the live final broadcast snapshot, rewrites the same assistant-media transcript entry when a later final replaces an earlier block, and keeps TTS spoken text out of the visible final/history copy.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS at head `b9c5e58dccb9658084a2058b6a83523ef158f63a`, based on `origin/main` `23e9bc8c0b61f37851136dc5da2311935e348f5d`. I ran the repo's connected Control UI Gateway server test, which starts a real local Gateway on `127.0.0.1`, uses a real local WebSocket client to send `chat.send`, and uses real local HTTP fetches against `http://127.0.0.1:<port>/__openclaw__/assistant-media` for metadata and ticketed media download.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
- `env COREPACK_HOME=/private/tmp/openclaw-corepack node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts -t "chat.send broadcasts agent-run TTS audio over the running gateway" --reporter verbose`
- The mocked agent dispatch starts an agent run in the running Gateway, sends a media-bearing `block` reply from `block.mp3`, then sends the final TTS reply from `tts.mp3`.
- The test waits for the WebSocket final `chat` event, calls the assistant-media metadata route, calls the ticketed media download route, reloads `chat.history`, and verifies history exposes the same final `tts.mp3` attachment rather than the earlier `block.mp3`.

Evidence after fix:
```txt
✓ |gateway-server| ../../src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts > gateway server chat > chat.send broadcasts agent-run TTS audio over the running gateway 384ms

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  1 passed | 29 skipped (30)

```

The focused handler regression also passed after the final patch:
```txt
✓ |gateway-client| ... persists agent-run audio replies emitted as media-bearing block payloads 99ms
✓ |gateway-client| ... persists auto-TTS final media as audio-only so webchat does not duplicate assistant text 25ms
✓ |gateway-client| ... broadcasts final agent media instead of an earlier media-bearing block 22ms

```

Observed result after fix: The WebSocket final event arrived with `runId: idem-running-gateway-agent-tts`, `sessionKey: agent:main:main`, and `state: final`. The final assistant content included `{ type: "text", text: "Audio reply" }` plus an audio attachment for the final `tts.mp3` path with `kind: "audio"`, `mimeType: "audio/mpeg"`, and `isVoiceNote: true`. The earlier `block.mp3` payload did not replace the final `tts.mp3` broadcast. The final WebSocket message did not contain the model transcript spoken text. The assistant-media metadata request returned HTTP 200 with `available: true` and a `v1.` media ticket; the ticketed media download returned HTTP 200 and 4 bytes. `chat.history` returned exactly one assistant message for the run, did not contain the spoken TTS text, and contained the same final `tts.mp3` attachment rather than `block.mp3`. The block-only media regression asserted no final broadcast was emitted from a media block by itself.

What was not tested: No real Microsoft/Xiaomi TTS credentials were used; the test uses a local MP3 fixture while exercising the actual Gateway WebSocket final event, assistant-media HTTP route, ticketed media download, transcript rewrite, and history dedupe path. Browser autoplay behavior was not tested. Local full prod/test typecheck was not runnable from this sparse checkout because tracked `packages` and `ui` inputs are not present here; GitHub Actions runs those checks in the full repository checkout.

target.title: fix(tasks): recover from corrupted task registry SQLite on startup
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Gateway startup fails with uncaught `ERR_SQLITE_ERROR: database disk image is malformed` when the task registry SQLite file (`tasks/runs.sqlite`) is corrupted, making the gateway unusable until the file is manually removed.
- Solution: Detect SQLite corruption errors during database open, quarantine the corrupted file (with sidecar -shm/-wal), and create a fresh database so the gateway can recover without manual intervention.
- What changed: `src/tasks/task-registry.store.sqlite.ts` — added `isSqliteCorruptionError()` detection and `quarantineCorruptedDatabase()` recovery in `openTaskRegistryDatabase()`.
- What did NOT change: No changes to task registry schema, API surface, or behavior when the database is healthy. No other components were affected.

Fixes #71689

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Gateway crashes on startup when `tasks/runs.sqlite` is corrupted (malformed disk image, garbage bytes, or other SQLite integrity errors).
- **Real environment tested:** Node 24.x on Linux, manual `node --import tsx` execution with corrupted SQLite file simulation.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  node --import tsx -e "
  import { writeFileSync, mkdirSync, existsSync, readdirSync } from 'node:fs';
  import { join } from 'node:path';
  const dir = '/tmp/proof-corruption-recovery';
  mkdirSync(dir, { recursive: true });
  const dbPath = join(dir, 'runs.sqlite');
  writeFileSync(dbPath, Buffer.from('garbage bytes simulating corrupted sqlite'));
  // Then open the database — the fix detects corruption, quarantines, and creates fresh db
  "
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from `node --import tsx` execution confirming corruption detection, quarantine, and fresh database recovery:

  ```text
  === Before Recovery ===
  Created corrupted file: /tmp/proof-corruption-recovery/runs.sqlite
  File exists: true

  Error detected: file is not a database
  Error code: ERR_SQLITE_ERROR
  Is corruption error: true

  Quarantined to: /tmp/proof-corruption-recovery/runs.sqlite.corrupted.1779765559823

  === After Recovery ===
  Fresh database created
  Rows in fresh db: [{"id":"task-1","value":"Post-recovery task"}]
  Quarantine file exists: true
  Fresh db exists: true
  Directory contents: runs.sqlite, runs.sqlite.corrupted.1779765559823
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** The `node` process detects the corrupted SQLite file, renames it to `runs.sqlite.corrupted.{timestamp}`, creates a fresh database in its place, and read/write roundtrip succeeds. Gateway continues startup normally with empty task state.
- **What was not tested:** Did not test recovery from corruption that occurs mid-operation (only tested startup recovery). Did not test on macOS or Windows.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: Unit test
- Target test file: `src/tasks/task-registry.store.test.ts`
- Scenario locked in: Two tests — (1) corrupted main db file triggers quarantine and fresh db creation, (2) corrupted db with WAL sidecar files triggers quarantine and fresh db creation. Both verify write-read roundtrip after recovery.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The tests directly write garbage bytes to simulate the exact corruption scenario from the issue report, then verify the recovery path creates a functional database.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `openTaskRegistryDatabase()` had no error handling around `new DatabaseSync()` and `ensureSchema()`. When the SQLite file is corrupted, `db.exec()` throws `ERR_SQLITE_ERROR` with message "file is not a database" or "database disk image is malformed", which propagated uncaught and crashed the gateway.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No corruption detection, no quarantine/recovery path, no graceful degradation for the task registry store.

target.title: fix(cli): add Windows stack-size respawn
target.body:
## Summary
- add a Windows-only respawn path that launches the CLI with `--stack-size=8192`
- normalize Windows argv before building the respawn child argv so duplicated `node.exe` / wrapper args are not forwarded
- preserve existing Windows behavior for CA cert and warning-suppression respawns
- skip the respawn when the user already provided a `--stack-size` exec arg
- pin non-Windows respawn baseline tests to Linux so Windows test runs exercise the new path deterministically

Fixes #62055.

## Validation
- WSL/Linux: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/entry.respawn.test.ts --reporter=dot` -> 16 tests passed
- WSL/Linux: `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/entry.respawn.ts src/entry.respawn.test.ts src/cli/windows-argv.ts`
- WSL/Linux: `./node_modules/.bin/oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/entry.respawn.ts src/entry.respawn.test.ts src/cli/windows-argv.ts`
- WSL/Linux: `git diff --check refs/remotes/upstream-safe/main...HEAD`
- WSL/Linux: `git diff --check`
- Windows 11 / Node v22.22.3: `node node_modules\vitest\vitest.mjs src/entry.respawn.test.ts --reporter=dot --run` -> 16 tests passed

Signed head: `21b89af215c6ebeb8d3d5466f1c66e7b0570f8a5`.

## ClawSweeper follow-up
- Addressed P1 `Normalize Windows argv before respawning` by calling `normalizeWindowsArgv()` from `buildCliRespawnPlan()` for `win32` before skip checks and before constructing child args.
- Added regression coverage for duplicated `node.exe` / wrapper args so the respawn child receives `--stack-size=8192`, the OpenClaw script path, and the real command args only.

## Windows packaged runtime proof

Head tested: `b139bf5db3f900ff694ac3e376855c84185091ed`.

Environment: Windows 11, Node `v22.22.3`, npm `10.9.8`, package tarball built from this head with `npm pack` after `node scripts/build-all.mjs`.

Local machine caveat: this Windows environment blocks Node from executing script filenames containing `openclaw` with `Access is denied`, even for a one-line test file. To avoid that local endpoint-policy block while still testing the packed runtime, I extracted the tarball under `C:\src\claws\ocpkg-86307\pkg`, installed production dependencies in that extracted package with `npm install --ignore-scripts --omit=dev --no-bin-links`, and invoked the packed `dist\entry.js` directly. This exercises the PR's Windows `buildCliRespawnPlan()` / `runCliRespawnPlan()` path without manually setting `NODE_OPTIONS`.

Exact proof commands/results:
- `node C:\src\claws\ocpkg-86307\pkg\dist\entry.js plugins list --json` with isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME` -> exit `0`; JSON output included `"plugins": []` and registry source `"derived"`.
- `node C:\src\claws\ocpkg-86307\pkg\dist\entry.js doctor` with isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME` -> exit `0`; emitted normal first-run doctor findings for missing gateway config, auth token, plugin registry, and state directory.
- `echo n | node C:\src\claws\ocpkg-86307\pkg\dist\entry.js dashboard --no-open` with isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME` -> exit `0`; emitted `Gateway is not running.` and suggested gateway status/start/run commands.
- `NODE_DEBUG=child_process node C:\src\claws\ocpkg-86307\pkg\dist\entry.js plugins list --json` -> exit `0`; debug output showed the Windows respawn args included `C:\Program Files\nodejs\node.exe`, `--stack-size=8192`, the packed `dist\entry.js`, and `plugins list --json`.

Remaining proof gap: the exact `openclaw.mjs`/`.cmd` filename could not be smoked on this corporate Windows machine because of the local filename policy described above. The packed runtime entry, dependency set, command behavior, and Windows stack-size respawn were exercised.

Note: commits are SSH-signed locally and verified with `git log --show-signature -1`.

target.title: fix(imessage): dedupe accounts sharing the local Messages source
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #65141. When `channels.imessage.accounts` contained both `default` and a named account pointing at the same local Messages source, the gateway started two `imsg rpc` watchers and inbound messages were processed twice (one `ping` yielded two `pong` replies).

This mirrors the Discord duplicate-token pattern (`extensions/discord/src/accounts.ts:172`) for iMessage:

- `resolveIMessageAccountSourceSignature(account)` derives a `(cliPath, dbPath)` signature for the local backend an account targets.
- `resolveIMessageAccountSourceOwner({ cfg, signature })` picks the canonical owner, **preferring an explicit named account over the implicit `default`** so bindings tied to a named account keep delivering.
- `isIMessageAccountEnabledForRuntime` / `resolveIMessageAccountDisabledReason` are wired into the iMessage channel config adapter, so the existing Gateway `startAccount` loop (`src/gateway/server-channels.ts:458-473`) skips non-owner duplicates and reports `duplicate iMessage source; using account "<owner>"` in runtime state.
- `imessageDoctor.collectPreviewWarnings` flags the redundant account from `openclaw doctor` with source coordinates and the `enabled: false` silencing tip. The doctor adapter moved from an inline `{ groupAllowFromFallbackToAllowFrom: false }` into a proper `extensions/imessage/src/doctor.ts` to host the new warning.

User config in the issue (`default` + `swang430-gmail-com`) now starts one `imsg rpc` and the named account keeps receiving the binding, matching the workaround the reporter applied manually.

## Verification

Behavior addressed: duplicate `imsg rpc` watchers and duplicate inbound replies when two enabled iMessage accounts share the local backend.

Real environment tested: synthetic via the new vitest cases; not a live iMessage replay.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/imessage
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/imessage/src/accounts.ts extensions/imessage/src/accounts.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/channel.ts extensions/imessage/src/doctor.ts extensions/imessage/src/doctor.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/shared.ts
pnpm format:check extensions/imessage/src/accounts.ts extensions/imessage/src/doctor.ts extensions/imessage/src/channel.ts extensions/imessage/src/shared.ts extensions/imessage/src/accounts.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/doctor.test.ts
node scripts/check-changelog-attributions.mjs
```

Evidence after fix: `Test Files 47 passed (47) / Tests 452 passed (452)` across the iMessage extension suite (includes the new collision cases in `accounts.test.ts` and `doctor.test.ts`). Typecheck, lint, formatting, and changelog attribution scans all exit clean.

Observed result after fix: `listEnabledIMessageAccounts(cfg)` returns one account per unique `(cliPath, dbPath)`, the non-owner reports `duplicate iMessage source; using account "<owner>"`, and `imessageDoctor.collectPreviewWarnings` emits one `- channels.imessage: accounts "<owner>" and "<dup>" watch the same local Messages source (cliPath=imsg)...` line per collision group; no warning when only one account is enabled or when each account targets a distinct cliPath.

What was not tested: a live Mac with two configured iMessage accounts running `imsg rpc` end-to-end. Not local Crabbox-validated; broader changed lanes are deferred to CI.

## Notes

- No SDK contract or config schema change. Existing iMessage configs continue to load without migration.
- The owner-preference rule keeps explicit account ids working when paired with `default`. Reverse case (two named accounts targeting the same backend) still dedupes deterministically via `listIMessageAccountIds` order.
- CHANGELOG entry placed under `## Unreleased` § Fixes, thanking @swang430 who reported the original issue with a complete repro.

target.title: fix(gateway): dedupe probe warnings by gateway identity
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes #69135 by suppressing `multiple_gateways` only when reachable probe targets report the same provable gateway self identity.
- Gateway self presence now exposes a process-level `instanceId`; same-host/same-config ambiguity without a process/device identity remains conservative and still warns.
- SSH tunnel, proxy/TLS URL, and configured remote URL can resolve to the same gateway even when transport ports differ.
- Docs and regression tests now describe distinct, same, and identity-ambiguous gateway cases.

target.title: fix(webchat): broadcast agent TTS media final payloads
target.body:
## Summary
- Fixes #81468.
- Bridges WebChat-managed agent-run TTS/media supplements into the final `chat` broadcast payload, so the live WebChat path receives the audio attachment that was already appended to the transcript.
- Keeps media-bearing `block` replies from being saved as the eventual final broadcast snapshot; only actual `final` deliveries can populate that final media message.
- When a final media reply follows an earlier media block, rewrites the same assistant-media transcript entry so live final delivery and `chat.history` reload show the same final audio attachment.
- Adds running Gateway proof that sends `chat.send` over WebSocket, first delivers a media block, then delivers the final TTS media reply, verifies the assistant-media HTTP route can serve the MP3, and confirms `chat.history` has the same final audio without duplicate assistant entries.

## Verification
- `env COREPACK_HOME=/private/tmp/openclaw-corepack node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts -t "agent.*media|auto-TTS final media|media-bearing block" --reporter verbose`
  - `Test Files 2 passed (2)`
  - `Tests 10 passed | 166 skipped (176)`
  - `Duration 6.57s`
- `env COREPACK_HOME=/private/tmp/openclaw-corepack node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts -t "chat.send broadcasts agent-run TTS audio over the running gateway" --reporter verbose`
  - `Test Files 1 passed (1)`
  - `Tests 1 passed | 29 skipped (30)`
  - `Duration 4.89s`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Agent-run auto-TTS/audio replies could be persisted as a WebChat assistant-media transcript supplement while the live final WebChat `chat` event omitted the audio attachment. A media-bearing `block` delivery could also be captured as the final media broadcast candidate and mask a later real final media reply. This patch keeps block-media transcript compatibility, only lets actual final media deliveries populate the live final broadcast snapshot, rewrites the same assistant-media transcript entry when a later final replaces an earlier block, and keeps TTS spoken text out of the visible final/history copy.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS at head `b9c5e58dccb9658084a2058b6a83523ef158f63a`, based on `origin/main` `23e9bc8c0b61f37851136dc5da2311935e348f5d`. I ran the repo's connected Control UI Gateway server test, which starts a real local Gateway on `127.0.0.1`, uses a real local WebSocket client to send `chat.send`, and uses real local HTTP fetches against `http://127.0.0.1:<port>/__openclaw__/assistant-media` for metadata and ticketed media download.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
- `env COREPACK_HOME=/private/tmp/openclaw-corepack node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts -t "chat.send broadcasts agent-run TTS audio over the running gateway" --reporter verbose`
- The mocked agent dispatch starts an agent run in the running Gateway, sends a media-bearing `block` reply from `block.mp3`, then sends the final TTS reply from `tts.mp3`.
- The test waits for the WebSocket final `chat` event, calls the assistant-media metadata route, calls the ticketed media download route, reloads `chat.history`, and verifies history exposes the same final `tts.mp3` attachment rather than the earlier `block.mp3`.

Evidence after fix:
```txt
✓ |gateway-server| ../../src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts > gateway server chat > chat.send broadcasts agent-run TTS audio over the running gateway 384ms

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  1 passed | 29 skipped (30)
Duration  4.89s
```

The focused handler regression also passed after the final patch:
```txt
✓ |gateway-client| ... persists agent-run audio replies emitted as media-bearing block payloads 99ms
✓ |gateway-client| ... persists auto-TTS final media as audio-only so webchat does not duplicate assistant text 25ms
✓ |gateway-client| ... broadcasts final agent media instead of an earlier media-bearing block 22ms
✓ |gateway-methods| ... persists agent-run audio replies emitted as media-bearing block payloads 98ms
✓ |gateway-methods| ... persists auto-TTS final media as audio-only so webchat does not duplicate assistant text 28ms
✓ |gateway-methods| ... broadcasts final agent media instead of an earlier media-bearing block 48ms

Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  10 passed | 166 skipped (176)
Duration  6.57s
```

Observed result after fix: The WebSocket final event arrived with `runId: idem-running-gateway-agent-tts`, `sessionKey: agent:main:main`, and `state: final`. The final assistant content included `{ type: "text", text: "Audio reply" }` plus an audio attachment for the final `tts.mp3` path with `kind: "audio"`, `mimeType: "audio/mpeg"`, and `isVoiceNote: true`. The earlier `block.mp3` payload did not replace the final `tts.mp3` broadcast. The final WebSocket message did not contain the model transcript spoken text. The assistant-media metadata request returned HTTP 200 with `available: true` and a `v1.` media ticket; the ticketed media download returned HTTP 200 and 4 bytes. `chat.history` returned exactly one assistant message for the run, did not contain the spoken TTS text, and contained the same final `tts.mp3` attachment rather than `block.mp3`. The block-only media regression asserted no final broadcast was emitted from a media block by itself.

What was not tested: No real Microsoft/Xiaomi TTS credentials were used; the test uses a local MP3 fixture while exercising the actual Gateway WebSocket final event, assistant-media HTTP route, ticketed media download, transcript rewrite, and history dedupe path. Browser autoplay behavior was not tested. Local full prod/test typecheck was not runnable from this sparse checkout because tracked `packages` and `ui` inputs are not present here; GitHub Actions runs those checks in the full repository checkout.

## AI Assistance Disclosure
This PR was prepared with AI assistance. I reviewed the changed code path and verified the targeted tests above.

## Summary
