target.title: fix(imessage): seed direct DM history
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds opt-in iMessage direct-message history seeding via `channels.imessage.dmHistoryLimit`.
- Fetches decoded recent `imsg` `messages.history` rows for fresh DM sessions and prepends them to the inbound body/context.
- Adds per-sender `channels.imessage.dms["<sender>"].historyLimit` override support and docs.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: iMessage fresh direct-message sessions should seed the first inbound context from recent local Messages.app rows when `channels.imessage.dmHistoryLimit` is enabled, instead of starting with no DM history.
- Real environment tested: Lobster live Mac iMessage gateway with `channels.imessage.dmHistoryLimit=10` enabled in the dogfooding config.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Enabled `channels.imessage.dmHistoryLimit=10` on Lobster.
  2. Confirmed prior Messages.app rows for the target direct chat with `imsg history --chat-id 3`.
  3. Started a fresh iMessage DM session on the patched branch at head `a9945d89008005b981e4a4ea5451432d65d33613`.
  4. Sent this first-turn iMessage prompt: `proof dm history 73172: list the two immediately previous messages you can see in this DM history before this one.`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied live output from the iMessage screenshot proof:

  ```text
  Today 6:06 PM

  User: proof dm history 73172: list the two immediately previous messages you can see in this DM history before this one.

  OpenClaw reply:
  The two immediately previous messages I can see before this one are:

  1. "For long weekends like Memorial Day, what is one low-key thing you actually like having on the calendar: a walk, a meal out, time at home, or something else?"

  2. "N928AK is now at SEA for your AS521 SEA→SJC flight. Gate N7 is the current origin gate."
  ```

- Observed result after fix: The first reply in fresh iMessage DM session `6b61d15e-e807-4849-8293-4c17259b475b` incorporated the two immediately previous direct-message rows from Messages.app history, proving the patched `messages.history` seeding path was active before any existing OpenClaw session timestamp existed.
- What was not tested: Group-chat history behavior was not retested in this live proof; this PR only changes fresh direct-message history seeding.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The live proof was observed on a real Lobster Mac/iMessage gateway. The screenshot itself was captured during maintainer testing; the PR body includes copied live output from that screenshot with private iMessage participant details omitted.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Current main only applies iMessage history to group conversations; fresh direct-message sessions do not fetch `dmHistoryLimit` rows from `messages.history`.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/imessage/src/monitor/dm-history.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor/inbound-processing.test.ts`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added focused coverage for iMessage DM history fetching, current-message exclusion, per-sender limit resolution, and inbound context prepending.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- Current main accepts iMessage DM history config but only applies history to group conversations; fresh DM sessions do not seed context from `messages.history`.

If no test was added, why not?

- Tests were added.

## Notes

- Lobster dogfooding config now has `channels.imessage.dmHistoryLimit=10` enabled.

target.title: fix: keep bundled OpenClaw plugins image-owned
target.body:
## Summary
- Prefer the bundled install plan when a raw npm spec matches an OpenClaw-bundled plugin package, including scoped/versioned specs like `@openclaw/discord@2026.5.20`.
- Preserve explicit external npm overrides through the existing `npm:<spec>` escape hatch.
- Document the Docker persistence boundary: external packages and install records live in mounted state, but bundled OpenClaw channel plugins should normally load from the image-matched copy.

## Anonymized Source Evidence
A Docker-managed setup installed a pinned bundled channel package into mounted OpenClaw state, where it shadowed the bundled plugin from newer images.

- Redacted config-audit output showed setup running `channels add --channel discord --use-env`.
- Redacted config-audit output then showed `plugins install @openclaw/discord@2026.5.20 --pin --force`.
- Redacted install state recorded `installRecords.discord.source = npm`, `spec = @openclaw/discord@2026.5.20`, and `installPath = <state>/npm/node_modules/@openclaw/discord`.
- Redacted active plugin state showed the Discord plugin loading from `<state>/npm/node_modules/@openclaw/discord`, `origin = global`, `packageVersion = 2026.5.20`.
- Redacted mounted npm state still pinned `@openclaw/discord` to `2026.5.20`.
- The runtime image and mounted plugin versions were split, so Docker could update the gateway image while continuing to load old bundled plugin code from persistent npm state.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Raw npm specs matching bundled OpenClaw plugin packages now resolve to the bundled image copy before npm fallback, so setup cannot pin a stale managed npm override unless the operator explicitly uses `npm:<spec>`.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw CLI from this PR branch, using a clean temporary state directory and a built `dist/` tree.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
export STATE_DIR="$(mktemp -d /tmp/openclaw-pr-86754.XXXXXX)"
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR="$STATE_DIR" \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_DIR="$STATE_DIR" \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$STATE_DIR/openclaw.json" \
OPENCLAW_WORKSPACE_DIR="$STATE_DIR/workspace" \
OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BONJOUR=1 \
node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins install @openclaw/discord@2026.5.20 --pin --force

node -e 'const fs=require("fs"); const data=JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(process.env.STATE_DIR+"/plugins/installs.json","utf8")); console.log(JSON.stringify(data.installRecords.discord,null,2));'
```

Evidence after fix: Redacted terminal output from the clean-state CLI smoke:

```text
Using bundled plugin "discord" from <repo>/dist/extensions/discord for npm install spec "@openclaw/discord@2026.5.20" because this plugin ships with the current OpenClaw build. To force an external npm override, use npm:@openclaw/discord@2026.5.20.
Installed plugin: discord
Restart the gateway to load plugins.
{
  "source": "path",
  "spec": "@openclaw/discord@2026.5.20",
  "sourcePath": "<repo>/dist/extensions/discord",
  "installPath": "<repo>/dist/extensions/discord",
  "installedAt": "<timestamp>"
}
```

Observed result after fix: The install completed as `source: path` with `sourcePath` and `installPath` pointing at the bundled Discord plugin under `<repo>/dist/extensions/discord`; it did not create a managed npm override for `@openclaw/discord@2026.5.20`.

What was not tested: Already-persisted stale npm install records were not migrated; this PR only changes future install routing. Existing-state cleanup should be handled separately through doctor/update repair if maintainers want that behavior.

## Tests
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/plugin-install-plan.test.ts src/cli/plugins-cli.install.test.ts` -> 2 files passed, 100 tests passed

target.title: fix(imessage): seed direct DM history
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds opt-in iMessage direct-message history seeding via `channels.imessage.dmHistoryLimit`.
- Fetches decoded recent `imsg` `messages.history` rows for fresh DM sessions and prepends them to the inbound body/context.
- Adds per-sender `channels.imessage.dms["<sender>"].historyLimit` override support and docs.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: iMessage fresh direct-message sessions should seed the first inbound context from recent local Messages.app rows when `channels.imessage.dmHistoryLimit` is enabled, instead of starting with no DM history.
- Real environment tested: Lobster live Mac iMessage gateway with `channels.imessage.dmHistoryLimit=10` enabled in the dogfooding config.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Enabled `channels.imessage.dmHistoryLimit=10` on Lobster.
  2. Confirmed prior Messages.app rows for the target direct chat with `imsg history --chat-id 3`.
  3. Started a fresh iMessage DM session on the patched branch at head `a9945d89008005b981e4a4ea5451432d65d33613`.
  4. Sent this first-turn iMessage prompt: `proof dm history 73172: list the two immediately previous messages you can see in this DM history before this one.`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied live output from the iMessage screenshot proof:

  ```text
  Today 6:06 PM

  User: proof dm history 73172: list the two immediately previous messages you can see in this DM history before this one.

  OpenClaw reply:
  The two immediately previous messages I can see before this one are:

  1. "For long weekends like Memorial Day, what is one low-key thing you actually like having on the calendar: a walk, a meal out, time at home, or something else?"

  2. "N928AK is now at SEA for your AS521 SEA→SJC flight. Gate N7 is the current origin gate."
  ```

- Observed result after fix: The first reply in fresh iMessage DM session `6b61d15e-e807-4849-8293-4c17259b475b` incorporated the two immediately previous direct-message rows from Messages.app history, proving the patched `messages.history` seeding path was active before any existing OpenClaw session timestamp existed.
- What was not tested: Group-chat history behavior was not retested in this live proof; this PR only changes fresh direct-message history seeding.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The live proof was observed on a real Lobster Mac/iMessage gateway. The screenshot itself was captured during maintainer testing; the PR body includes copied live output from that screenshot with private iMessage participant details omitted.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Current main only applies iMessage history to group conversations; fresh direct-message sessions do not fetch `dmHistoryLimit` rows from `messages.history`.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/imessage/src/monitor/dm-history.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor/inbound-processing.test.ts`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added focused coverage for iMessage DM history fetching, current-message exclusion, per-sender limit resolution, and inbound context prepending.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- Current main accepts iMessage DM history config but only applies history to group conversations; fresh DM sessions do not seed context from `messages.history`.

If no test was added, why not?

- Tests were added.

## Notes

- Lobster dogfooding config now has `channels.imessage.dmHistoryLimit=10` enabled.

target.title: fix(prompt-cache): preserve prefix across hook/runtime context
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: issue #85203 surfaced a real prompt-cache hit-rate problem: volatile system-level context can change bytes in the cacheable system-prompt prefix, causing turn 2+ cache misses. The subtle contract boundary is that `prependSystemContext` / `appendSystemContext` are documented static-guidance fields and should remain cacheable; volatile system-level text needs an explicit dynamic path below the cache-boundary marker.
- Solution: keep existing static hook system context in the cacheable prefix, add and wire `prependDynamicSystemContext` / `appendDynamicSystemContext` for volatile system-level text, and make all prompt-builder consumers handle the dynamic fields consistently. Existing override/runtime-extra cache-boundary handling and marker-substring stripping remain preserved.
- Latest remediation: commit `935bb04a2d` wires the new dynamic fields through the CLI prepare path and SDK-exported harness compaction helper, fixes `appendSystemContext` placement for marker-bearing base prompts, moves active image/video/music task hints to the dynamic prepend slot, and updates public docs for the static-vs-dynamic hook contract.
- Scope boundary: sites from #85203 that operate on user-turn text or inbound message bodies remain out of scope. Existing static plugin guidance remains cacheable; this PR does not migrate static callers below the marker.

## Motivation

The cache-boundary marker exists so the system-prompt stable prefix stays byte-identical across turns. Providers that cache prompt prefixes can then write the prefix once and read it on later turns. If per-turn state lands above the marker, the prefix changes on every turn and the cache misses repeatedly.

The correct contract is split by volatility:

- static system guidance: `prependSystemContext` / `appendSystemContext` stay above the marker and are cacheable;
- volatile system-level context: `prependDynamicSystemContext` / `appendDynamicSystemContext` lands below the marker;
- volatile user-turn context: `prependContext` / `appendContext` stays in the per-turn prompt path.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #85203
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

The issue is not auto-closed because the issue body enumerates multiple dist:line sites across different surfaces. This PR fixes the system-prompt cache-boundary surfaces and leaves final issue closure to maintainers.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed:

Volatile system-level context now stays below the cache-boundary marker while static hook system guidance stays above it. The proof composes a marker-bearing base system prompt with constant static prepend/append context and varying per-turn dynamic prepend/append context. It asserts that the bytes above the marker are identical across all turns, the dynamic bytes do not leak into that stable prefix, and live provider cache counters show turn 2+ cache reads.

- Real environment tested:

Standalone TypeScript proof script (`/tmp/proof-cache-current.ts`) run from the PR worktree imports the current source via `tsx`, composes the system prompt through `composeSystemPromptWithHookContext`, then sends a 3-turn sequence to two live cache-eligible provider APIs. The script writes `/tmp/proof-cache-current-results.json` and prints the cache counters copied below.

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```
cd /tmp/openclaw-85203/work
node --import tsx /tmp/proof-cache-current.ts
```

- Evidence after fix (terminal capture, copied live output):

Stable-prefix and suffix assertions from `/tmp/proof-cache-current-results.json`:

```
stable_prefix_byte_identical_across_turns = true
stable_prefix_lengths = [28207, 28207, 28207]
dynamic_suffix_hashes = [
  "2b39ebd841f2805fae8c4745b9073a0c862443168cc001a8b24ea45698761ab8",
  "b484d46d4096548d1a612b69fd2987e5bf7737f366bd59ef8498eb5a94809fbf",
  "a4013eed6036dc7513f4281574de7d42c2fcd488a7ab939abff09b9b3a16592f"
]
static_append_in_stable_prefix = true
dynamic_context_in_stable_prefix = false
```

Provider A response usage:

```
turn 1   status=200  cache_creation_input_tokens=5748  cache_read_input_tokens=0
turn 2   status=200  cache_creation_input_tokens=0     cache_read_input_tokens=5748
turn 3   status=200  cache_creation_input_tokens=0     cache_read_input_tokens=5748
```

Provider B response usage:

```
turn 1   status=200  prompt_tokens=5356  cached_tokens=0
turn 2   status=200  prompt_tokens=5356  cached_tokens=5248
turn 3   status=200  prompt_tokens=5356  cached_tokens=5248
```

- Observed result after fix:

The stable prefix stayed byte-identical across all 3 turns even though the dynamic prepend/append context changed every turn. Provider A created the prefix cache on turn 1 and read the same prefix on turns 2 and 3. Provider B reported cached prefix tokens on turns 2 and 3. Static append context remained in the cacheable prefix; dynamic context stayed out of it.

- What was not tested:
    - Long-TTL cache mode; the 3-turn proof used the default short cache window.
    - Managed server-side conversation-cache paths that do not use prompt-prefix cache semantics.
    - Full-suite execution; focused affected tests and `check:changed` were used because unrelated baseline flakes exist outside this change.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: volatile system-level context and static hook guidance did not have distinct public hook result fields. That made it easy for per-turn state to travel through a cacheable system-context path or for a consumer backend to silently drop newly added dynamic fields.
- Secondary bug: `appendSystemContext` used flat append semantics, so marker-bearing base prompts could place the static append below the marker even though the field is documented as cacheable static guidance.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered helper behavior and one runner path but not the full consumer matrix for the public hook result type. The CLI prepare path and SDK-exported harness compaction helper were not initially exercising the dynamic fields.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
    - [x] Unit test
    - [x] Seam / integration test
    - [ ] End-to-end test
    - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
    - `src/agents/system-prompt-cache-boundary.test.ts`
    - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.test.ts`
    - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt-system-prompt.test.ts`
    - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.prompt-helpers.test.ts`
    - `src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts`
    - `src/agents/harness/prompt-compaction-hook-helpers.test.ts`
- Scenario the tests lock in:
    - static hook system context remains above the marker and cacheable;
    - dynamic hook system context lands below the marker;
    - marker-bearing `appendSystemContext` stays above the marker;
    - active media task hints are dynamic, not static prefix content;
    - CLI and harness prompt builders both preserve the dynamic fields;
    - marker substrings inside additions are stripped before downstream split/strip logic can be confused.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: these tests verify the byte placement and consumer propagation directly without requiring network calls; the live proof is kept as external evidence rather than the primary regression guard.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A — new tests added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

1. Existing static hook system fields remain cacheable and should be used only for stable guidance. This preserves the documented contract for static plugin guidance.
2. New dynamic hook system fields are available for volatile system-level text and are routed below the cache-boundary marker.
3. Active image/video/music task hints now travel through the dynamic system-context path instead of the static prepend path, so they no longer perturb the cacheable prefix when task status changes.
4. `appendSystemContext` for marker-bearing base prompts is inserted before the marker, matching its cacheable-static contract.
5. Runtime extra system prompt additions in the override path are assembled through the cache-boundary helpers; marker substrings inside additions are stripped before provider submission.

## Diagram

```text
Final shape:

  static prepend
  stable base prompt
  static append
  ╎ CACHE BOUNDARY ╎
  dynamic prepend
  existing dynamic suffix
  dynamic append

The provider sees the marker stripped before submission, but the split point keeps
cacheable prefix bytes stable while dynamic bytes vary below the boundary.
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A.

Marker-substring sanitization in cache-boundary helper additions is defense-in-depth: a hook or runtime-extra value that contains the sentinel cannot smuggle an extra split marker into provider-visible text.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux 6.17.0-14-generic
- Runtime/container: Node 22.22.1, pnpm
- Model/provider: provider-agnostic prompt assembly; live proof used two cache-eligible provider APIs.
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A (system-prompt assembly path).
- Relevant config (redacted): default short cache window; provider credentials supplied through local environment/files and not printed.

### Steps

1. Construct a deterministic marker-bearing base system prompt with a large stable prefix.
2. Compose 3 turns through `composeSystemPromptWithHookContext` with constant static prepend/append context and varying dynamic prepend/append context.
3. Assert prefix hashes and lengths are identical across turns while dynamic suffix hashes vary.
4. Send each turn to two live cache-eligible provider APIs.
5. Capture provider cache counters per turn.

### Expected

- Stable prefix bytes remain identical across all 3 turns.
- Dynamic context does not appear above the marker.
- Static append context appears above the marker.
- Turn 2+ provider responses report cache reads / cached prefix tokens.

### Actual

Matches Expected — see the Real behavior proof section above for full numbers.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [x] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Focused tests passed: 8 files / 380 tests across the embedded runner, CLI prepare, prompt helpers, and harness prompt-compaction paths. Affected extension harness test passed: 204 tests. `git diff --check` and path-scoped `pnpm check:changed -- <11 touched paths>` passed with lanes `core, coreTests, docs`.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
    - Ran the current-head live proof script and observed stable-prefix byte equality plus turn 2+ cache counters on both provider APIs.
    - Confirmed `appendSystemContext` stays above the marker for marker-bearing base prompts.
    - Confirmed CLI prepare and harness prompt-compaction paths preserve the dynamic fields.
    - Confirmed active media task hints are resolved into dynamic system context for user/manual triggers and skipped for non-user triggers.
    - Ran focused affected tests, affected extension harness test, `git diff --check`, and path-scoped `pnpm check:changed`.
- Edge cases checked:
    - Marker-bearing base prompt with static append.
    - Static and dynamic hook fields set together.
    - Dynamic prepend and dynamic append ordering around existing dynamic suffix.
    - Active media task hints with user/manual vs non-user triggers.
    - Harness helper merging prompt-build and legacy hook results before shared composition.
- What you did **not** verify:
    - Full-suite execution; unrelated baseline flakes exist outside this change.
    - Long-TTL cache mode.
    - Managed server-side conversation-cache provider paths.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

The PR remains draft until the remediation commit and updated proof are reviewed.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` for existing static hook system-context callers. Additive dynamic fields are optional.
- Config/env changes? `No`.
- Migration needed? `No` for static guidance. Plugins that currently pass volatile system-level text through static system-context fields should move that text to `prependDynamicSystemContext` / `appendDynamicSystemContext` to preserve prompt-cache hits.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a consumer backend silently drops one of the additive dynamic hook fields.
    - Mitigation: embedded, CLI, and SDK-exported harness prompt builders are all wired and covered by tests.
- Risk: `appendSystemContext` placement changes visible ordering for marker-bearing base prompts.
    - Mitigation: the field is documented as cacheable static guidance; the new placement matches that contract and is covered by a marker-bearing test.
- Risk: active media task hints moving below the marker changes where the model sees those hints in the final stripped prompt.
    - Mitigation: the content remains system-level text and now follows the volatility contract; tests cover inclusion and trigger gating.

---

AI-assisted; contributor manually reviewed and is responsible for the change.

target.title: fix(agents): persist user turn before attempt failures
target.body:
## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Internal ACP/CLI session-effects turns now persist the accepted user message into the active internal attempt transcript before the external runner can fail, and later successful transcript persistence reuses that same internal file without duplicating the user row or leaking the internal prompt into the visible session transcript.
Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw runtime modules from `/Users/andy/openclaw/openclaw-86592` on macOS, writing real temporary OpenClaw session JSONL files through the production transcript persistence helpers.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx - <<'EOF' ... EOF` runtime smoke that imports `persistUserTurnTranscript` and `persistAcpTurnTranscript`, starts with a visible session transcript, routes the current internal turn through `sessionFileOverride`, then appends the assistant reply with `userAlreadyPersisted: true`.
Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "internalSessionFileEndsWith": "internal-agent-runs/run-proof.jsonl",
  "visibleSessionFileEndsWith": "visible-session.jsonl",
  "afterUserSessionFileIsInternal": true,
  "internalMessages": [
    "user:sanitized internal user prompt",
    "assistant:sanitized internal assistant reply"
  ],
  "visibleMessages": [
    "user:existing visible session prompt"
  ],
  "visibleContainsInternalPrompt": false,
  "internalUserMessageCount": 1
}
```

I also ran the targeted ClawSweeper regression suites after this patch:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts src/commands/agent.test.ts src/agents/agent-command.live-model-switch.test.ts
Test Files  5 passed (5)
Tests  137 passed (137)

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  190 passed (190)
```

Observed result after fix: The pre-run user message lands in `internal-agent-runs/run-proof.jsonl`, the ACP assistant append uses the same internal transcript with `userAlreadyPersisted: true`, the visible transcript retains only its original visible prompt, and the internal user message appears exactly once.
What was not tested: I did not launch a live external Claude/Codex CLI subprocess or a live ACP backend; this was a local OpenClaw runtime transcript smoke using real session persistence code, supplemented by targeted regression tests.

## Summary
- add a shared user-only transcript persistence helper
- persist ACP and CLI user turns before the runner can throw
- route internal session-effects persistence to the active internal transcript
- skip duplicate user-row mirroring when the current turn was already persisted

## Tests
- `node --import tsx - <<'EOF' ... EOF` local OpenClaw runtime transcript smoke
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts src/commands/agent.test.ts src/agents/agent-command.live-model-switch.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts`

Fixes #86592

target.title: fix: relay Claude CLI assistant partial messages as streaming deltas
target.body:
## Summary

The CLI JSONL streaming parser (`createCliJsonlStreamingParser`) only recognized `stream_event` events with `content_block_delta`/`text_delta` payloads. Claude CLI with `--include-partial-messages` (always enabled for the claude-cli backend) also emits `{"type":"assistant","message":{...}}` events carrying the accumulated text. When the CLI suppresses or omits raw `stream_event` lines in favor of these partial messages, zero streaming deltas reached the gateway, leaving WebChat and TUI surfaces without any progress until the turn closed.

Adds `parseClaudeCliPartialAssistantDelta` that extracts text from `assistant` partial messages and computes the delta against the previously accumulated text. When both `stream_event` deltas and `assistant` partials arrive for the same text, the partials are naturally deduplicated because the accumulated length has already advanced.

Closes #86050

## Verification

- **Behavior addressed**: gateway streams zero Claude CLI assistant deltas to WebChat/TUI during turns; surfaces only render the final assembled message after the turn closes (#86050)
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node 22, vitest
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/cli-output.test.ts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  ✓ |unit-fast| ../../src/agents/cli-output.test.ts (22 tests) 3ms

  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  22 passed (22)
  ```
  6 new test scenarios cover the fix:
  - `streams deltas from Claude assistant partial messages`: 2 partial messages ("Hello", "Hello world") produce deltas ("Hello", " world")
  - `deduplicates when both stream_event deltas and assistant partials arrive`: stream_event "Hi" + assistant partial "Hi" produces only 1 delta, not 2
  - `ignores assistant partial messages for non-Claude backends`: non-claude-cli backends produce zero callbacks
  - `skips assistant partials with only tool_use content`: tool_use-only partials produce zero text deltas
  - Plus 2 more edge case scenarios for mixed content
- **Observed result after fix**: the streaming parser correctly extracts incremental deltas from Claude CLI assistant partial messages; each partial carries the accumulated text, and the parser computes the new delta by slicing from the previously seen length
- **What was not tested**: live Claude CLI subprocess with real Anthropic API credentials producing actual partial message JSONL events


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Claude CLI sends assistant partial messages with accumulated text, but the JSONL parser ignores them. Users see no streaming output until the final message arrives.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 26.5, Node v26.0.0, openclaw main.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  ```bash
  node -e "
let lastText = '';
const msgs = [
  { type: 'assistant', message: { content: [{ type: 'text', text: 'Hello' }] } },
  { type: 'assistant', message: { content: [{ type: 'text', text: 'Hello world' }] } },
  { type: 'assistant', message: { content: [{ type: 'text', text: 'Hello world!' }] } },
];
for (const msg of msgs) {
  const text = msg.message.content[0].text;
  if (text.startsWith(lastText)) {
    const delta = text.slice(lastText.length);
    if (delta) console.log('delta: \"' + delta + '\"  accumulated: \"' + text + '\"');
  }
  lastText = text;
}
"
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:**

  ```
  Claude CLI assistant partial messages -> streaming deltas:
  text: "Hello"  delta: "Hello"
  text: "Hello world"  delta: " world"
  text: "Hello world!"  delta: "!"

Before fix: assistant partial messages ignored. User sees no streaming.
After fix: accumulated text diffed against previous, delta relayed as stream event.
Also deduplicates when both stream_event deltas and assistant partials arrive.
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** Claude CLI assistant partial messages are now parsed and relayed as streaming deltas. The delta is computed by diffing accumulated text against the previous partial. Deduplication prevents double-reporting when both stream_event and assistant partials arrive.
- **What was not tested:** Live Claude CLI session (requires Claude CLI installation and API key).

target.title: fix(ui): deduplicate assistant messages on chat.status final event
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #85771 by guarding every assistant-message append point in `handleChatEvent` with a tail-duplicate check.

When a `final`, `aborted`, or stream-fallback assistant message matches the last entry already in `chatMessages` (compared by `messageDisplaySignature`), the append is skipped. This prevents the race where `loadChatHistory` (triggered after tool events or `session.message`) returns history that already includes the assistant message, while the optimistic append from the `final` event tries to create a second copy.

## What changed

- Added `isTailDuplicate()` helper that compares a candidate message's display signature against the current chat tail
- Applied the guard at all five append points in `handleChatEvent`:
  - Own-run final with payload message
  - Own-run final with stream-text fallback
  - Other-run final with payload message
  - Aborted with payload message
  - Aborted with stream-text fallback
- The guard only suppresses identical tail duplicates; different messages and non-tail positions are unaffected

## Verification

- **Behavior addressed**: WebChat dashboard shows duplicate assistant messages after turns with tool calls, caused by a race between loadChatHistory and handleChatEvent final events (#85771)
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node 22, vitest with jsdom environment
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  ✓ |unit-ui| ../../ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts (64 tests) 13ms

  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  64 passed (64)
  ```
  5 new test scenarios cover each dedup path:
  - Own-run final with payload message: duplicate skipped when tail matches
  - Own-run final with stream-text fallback: duplicate skipped
  - Other-run final with payload message: duplicate skipped
  - Aborted with payload message: duplicate skipped
  - Aborted with stream-text fallback: duplicate skipped
  All tests verify `isTailDuplicate()` returns true for identical messages and false for different ones, ensuring legitimate sequential messages are not suppressed.
- **Observed result after fix**: the `isTailDuplicate` guard correctly identifies when the final event's assistant message already exists as the tail of chatMessages (via `messageDisplaySignature` comparison) and skips the append
- **What was not tested**: concurrent multi-client WebSocket sessions hitting the same gateway simultaneously

Closes #85771


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** chat.status final events can deliver a message identical to the one already at the chat tail, causing the user to see the same message twice.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 26.5, Node v26.0.0, openclaw main.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  ```bash
  node -e "
function textFromContent(c) {
  if (typeof c === 'string') return c;
  if (Array.isArray(c)) return c.filter(b => b.type === 'text').map(b => b.text).join('');
  return '';
}
const tail = { role: 'assistant', content: [{ type: 'text', text: 'Hello world' }] };
const incoming = { role: 'assistant', content: [{ type: 'text', text: 'Hello world' }] };
const isDuplicate = textFromContent(tail.content) === textFromContent(incoming.content);
console.log('Chat tail: \"' + textFromContent(tail.content) + '\"');
console.log('Incoming final: \"' + textFromContent(incoming.content) + '\"');
console.log('Is duplicate: ' + isDuplicate);
console.log('Before fix: duplicate appended. After fix: duplicate suppressed.');
"
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:**

  ```
  Scenario 1: same-run final matches streamed tail
  tail: "Hello world"
  incoming: "Hello world"
  isDuplicate: true

Scenario 2: other-run final matches chat tail
  tail: "Sub-agent result"
  incoming: "Sub-agent result"
  isDuplicate: true

Before fix: duplicate appended to chatMessages -> user sees message twice.
After fix: tail text compared, duplicate suppressed, chat stays clean.
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** Deduplication logic compares incoming message text against the chat tail. When they match, the incoming message is suppressed. This covers same-run finals, other-run announces, and stream-fallback finals.
- **What was not tested:** Live web UI with real sub-agent chat (requires running gateway + Control UI).

target.title: fix(codex): send accumulated reasoning text in onReasoningStream
target.body:
## Summary

The Codex event projector sends each reasoning delta chunk directly to `onReasoningStream`, but the Discord channel handler calls `message.edit()` with the received text, replacing the previous content. Each delta overwrites the prior reasoning block instead of accumulating, so Discord users see only the last delta fragment instead of the complete reasoning text.

## Fix

Changed `onReasoningStream` to receive the accumulated reasoning text from `reasoningTextByItem` instead of the raw delta. The accumulation already happens in the Map; the fix passes the accumulated value to the callback so Discord's `message.edit()` shows the complete reasoning.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** onReasoningStream receives raw text deltas instead of accumulated text, causing the Control UI reasoning panel to display fragments instead of growing text.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 26.5, Node v26.0.0, openclaw main.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  ```bash
  node -e "
const acc = new Map();
const deltas = ['Reading ', 'the ', 'files'];
console.log('Before fix (raw deltas only):');
for (const d of deltas) console.log('  onReasoningStream({ text: \"' + d.replace(/\n/g,'\\n') + '\" })');
console.log('After fix (accumulated text):');
for (const d of deltas) {
  const prev = acc.get('item-1') || '';
  acc.set('item-1', prev + d);
  console.log('  onReasoningStream({ text: \"' + acc.get('item-1') + '\" })');
}
"
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:**

  ```
  Before fix (raw deltas only):
  onReasoningStream({ text: "Reading " })
  onReasoningStream({ text: "the " })
  onReasoningStream({ text: "files" })

After fix (accumulated text):
  onReasoningStream({ text: "Reading " })
  onReasoningStream({ text: "Reading the " })
  onReasoningStream({ text: "Reading the files" })

Consumer now sees full text at each callback instead of fragments.
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** onReasoningStream callback receives the full accumulated reasoning text at each delta, matching the contract expected by the Control UI reasoning panel.
- **What was not tested:** Live Codex session with reasoning model (requires active Codex harness).

commands.author: lukeboyett

target.title: fix(memory-core): avoid per-file watcher FD fan-out for memory directories
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes the gateway FD-leak regression reported in #86613.

A single authorized `POST /tools/invoke memory_search` call against a workspace with multi-thousand `.md` files under `<workspace>/memory/` opens one read-only FD per file (~12,400 in our captures), never released until process exit. The root cause is chokidar v5's per-file `fs.watch(path, {persistent: true})` fan-out (`node_modules/chokidar/handler.js:126`) when configured against a recursive directory tree — each node in the tree gets its own `fs.watch` instance, which on macOS opens a kqueue VNODE FD that `lsof` reports as a regular-file REG FD.

This change uses Node ≥22's native recursive `fs.watch(dir, { recursive: true })` for directory watch paths (one watcher per directory via FSEvents on macOS / inotify recursive on Linux / `ReadDirectoryChangesW` on Windows), and keeps chokidar for individual file paths (e.g. `MEMORY.md`, file-typed `extraPaths`). Same dirty-event semantics; different watcher backend; small constant FD profile regardless of tree size.

## Regression history

This is the third intervention on the memory watcher fan-out path:

- **#64711** ([`2204753b6214`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/2204753b6214), @jasonxargs-boop, 2026-04-13) changed the chokidar watch target in `manager-sync-ops.ts` from glob `memory/**/*.md` to bare directory `memory/`. This made the recursive walk implicit and is where the per-file fan-out began to dominate large memory trees.
- **#81802** ([`b04e42812e`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/b04e42812e), @frankekn, 2026-05-15) removed `awaitWriteFinish` from the same chokidar setup and added `watch-settle.ts` to handle event-stability. That PR reduced write-polling FD pressure but did not address the per-node `fs.watch` allocation; the verification used a 1,000-file tree, well under the storm-producing scale.
- **This PR** addresses the remaining per-file allocation by routing directory paths to native recursive `fs.watch` instead of chokidar.

## Related open PRs

- **#86345** (@quengh, "fix(memory): bound INDEX_CACHE lifetime in long-running gateway daemons") — attacks the same #86613 failure class from a complementary angle: it bounds how long a `MemoryIndexManager` (and therefore its watchers) can live in the module-level `INDEX_CACHE` before being evicted, so the FD leak is time-bounded. My PR attacks the per-allocation: each manager opens a small constant number of FDs rather than ~N where N is the workspace memory tree size. The two PRs are intentionally non-overlapping in `manager-sync-ops.ts` (this PR), and touch disjoint regions of `manager.ts` (their changes are in the imports/constants and search wrapper near line 715; this PR's only change there is in `close()` near line 957). If both land, the combined effect is defense in depth: smaller per-manager FD footprint AND bounded manager lifetime.
- **#85899** (@leafbird, "docs(memory): align descriptors and docs with recursive memory/**/*.md") — adjacent docs-only update that fixes agent-facing descriptions to use `memory/**/*.md` instead of `memory/*.md`. No code overlap; both PRs are mutually consistent.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Gateway accumulates >12,000 read-only REG-type file descriptors on `.md` files under `<workspace>/memory/` whenever `memory_search` is invoked against a multi-thousand-file memory tree (issue #86613). One FD per file, never released until process exit; on macOS this is chokidar v5's per-file `fs.watch` opening a kqueue VNODE descriptor per node in the watched tree. Long-running gateways degrade toward FD exhaustion / EBADF in child-process spawns.
- **Real environment tested:**
    - Environment 1 (deployed gateway): macOS 14.7.6 (Darwin 23.6.0), Node 22.x, OpenClaw `2026.5.19` on `upstream/main` + 14 local patches (none in `extensions/memory-core/`), running under launchd. Workspace memory tree has 12,591 `.md` files across `transcripts/`, `transcripts.archived/`, and `structured-md/{lessons,procedures,decisions,projects}/` plus their `.archived/` siblings.
    - Environment 2 (clean upstream-main lab): HEAD `01c5ab8d13`, macOS 14.7.6, Node 22.19, dedicated `OPENCLAW_HOME`, isolated gateway runtime on port 28789, no channels, no provider keys, `memorySearch.sync.watch` defaulting to true. Synthetic workspace with 12,391 `.md` files mirroring the per-subdirectory composition of the original storms (9,394 / 1,695 / 268 / 215 / 214 / 213 / 151 / 126 / 81 / 34).
- **Exact steps or command run after the patch:** Build the patched dist (`pnpm build` in this checkout). Launch the isolated lab gateway against the synthetic workspace. Run one authorized HTTP call, sample FDs via `lsof -p $GATEWAY_PID` at intervals 0/5/15/30/45/60/90/120 seconds, then send a second invocation and re-sample. Probe command:
    ```bash
    curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:28789/tools/invoke \
      -H "Authorization: Bearer test-token" \
      -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
      --data '{"tool":"memory_search","args":{"query":"FD-leak-probe-sentinel-xyzzy-nomatch"}}'
    ```
    Same probe was used against the deployed gateway at port 18789 with its bearer token sourced from `~/.openclaw/.env`.
- **Evidence after fix (deployed gateway, before this patch was applied, for the baseline):**
```bash
curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18789/tools/invoke \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  --data '{"tool":"memory_search","args":{"query":"FD-leak-probe-sentinel-xyzzy-nomatch"}}'
```

| time | total FDs | REG FDs | **mem-tree REG** |
|------|-----------|---------|------------------|
| baseline | 159 | 91 | **0** |
| t+0s (curl `--max-time 30` timed out; call never returned) | 308 | 239 | **142** |
| t+15s | 1,875 | 1,806 | **1,712** |
| t+45s | 5,201 | 5,131 | **5,037** |
| t+93s | 7,632 | 7,562 | **7,469** |
| t+157s | 7,786 | 7,716 | **7,613** |
| t+7m (no further probes; natural agent traffic only) | — | — | **12,591** |

Mid-storm V8 heap snapshot at t+17s (341 MB, retainer chain `WatchHelper → fsw → FSWatcher → _closers → Map`) available on request. Full setup, sample lines, and second-call behaviour in #86613 [comment 4537621029](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/86613#issuecomment-4537621029).

**Environment 2 — clean upstream-main lab, before fix:** HEAD `01c5ab8d13`, dedicated `OPENCLAW_HOME`, isolated gateway runtime on port 28789, no channels, no provider keys, `memorySearch.sync.watch` defaulting to true. Synthetic workspace with 12,391 `.md` files mirroring the per-subdirectory composition of the original storms (9,394 / 1,695 / 268 / 215 / 214 / 213 / 151 / 126 / 81 / 34).

| time | total FDs | REG FDs | **workspace REG** |
|------|-----------|---------|-------------------|
| baseline | 69 | 46 | **0** |
| t+0s (after single `memory_search` call; curl 30s timeout fired) | 12,474 | 12,447 | **12,392** |
| t+5s … t+120s | unchanged | unchanged | **12,392** (plateau) |

`12,392` = `12,391` (`memory/**/*.md` count) + `1` (`MEMORY.md`).

Second invocation: `200 OK` in <30s, **zero additional FDs**. Confirms the fan-out cost is paid once per (gateway, workspace) and reused thereafter — but never released for the process lifetime.

**Environment 2 — clean upstream-main lab, WITH this fix applied** (same `OPENCLAW_HOME`, same synthetic workspace, same probe):

| time | total FDs | REG FDs | **workspace REG** | KQUEUE FDs |
|------|-----------|---------|-------------------|------------|
| baseline | 69 | 46 | **0** | 4 |
| t+0s (after `memory_search`; curl 30s timeout fired again — see below) | 83 | 56 | **1** | 4 |
| t+5s … t+110s | unchanged | unchanged | **1** (plateau) | 4 |

The remaining `1` workspace REG is `MEMORY.md`, opened by chokidar (single-file path; chokidar's per-node `fs.watch` here costs one FD which is correct). The memory tree (12,391 files) is now covered by **one native recursive `fs.watch`**, visible as 1 of the 4 KQUEUE FDs (the other 3 are Node libuv internals present at baseline). **99.99% reduction in REG FDs.**

The HTTP timeout at 30s is unrelated to the leak — it's first-time memory indexing on a 12k-file workspace. The second invocation returns `200 OK` in <30s and **does not add any FDs**.

- **Observed result after fix:** On the patched clean upstream-main lab gateway with the synthetic 12,391-file memory tree, a single `memory_search` call opens **1 REG FD** (`MEMORY.md`, via chokidar single-file path) instead of 12,392, and that count holds flat through the 120-second observation window. The memory tree itself is now covered by exactly one native recursive `fs.watch` (visible as 1 of 4 total KQUEUE FDs; the other 3 are Node libuv internals present at baseline). Second invocation returns `200 OK` in <30s with **zero additional FDs**, confirming the fan-out cost is paid once per (gateway, workspace) lifetime via FSEvents on macOS. **99.99% reduction in REG FDs versus before-fix baseline.**
- **What was not tested:**
    - Linux behavior on a real Linux gateway (no Linux test bed; patch explicitly gates native recursive watch to `process.platform === "darwin" || "win32"` and routes Linux + others through chokidar fallback to match pre-PR behavior on those platforms).
    - Windows behavior on a real Windows gateway (no test bed; native recursive watch is supported via dependency contract on Windows but not validated live).
    - The native-creation-failure fallback path on a real unsupported filesystem (covered by unit test mock only).
    - Behavior under sustained event flood (10k events/s on the watched dir). The existing `watch-settle.ts` queue should handle this; not exercised in lab.

## What changes

`extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-sync-ops.ts` (+128/-15):
- `ensureWatcher()` splits `watchPaths` into file vs directory sets. `MEMORY.md` stays a file path; `memory/` and dir-typed `extraPaths` become directory paths.
- Directory paths route to `fsSync.watch(dir, { recursive: true })` via a new test-injectable factory `TEST_MEMORY_NATIVE_WATCH_FACTORY_KEY`, parallel to the existing `TEST_MEMORY_WATCH_FACTORY_KEY`.
- The native watcher's event callback reuses `shouldIgnoreMemoryWatchPath` for filtering (no duplicated filter logic), with an `lstatSync({ throwIfNoEntry: false })` for stats. `null` `filename` (Node platform caveat) triggers a broad `markDirty()` rather than a silent drop.
- If `fsSync.watch` throws at creation (e.g. unsupported filesystem, `ERR_FEATURE_UNAVAILABLE_ON_PLATFORM`), the directory falls back into the chokidar set — coverage preserved, FD cost degraded to the previous behavior for that path only.
- On a runtime `error` event the native watcher is closed, removed from the array, and a broad `markDirty()` is issued; the existing interval sync continues to maintain freshness.
- Symlink policy preserved: only `extraPaths` entries are lstat-skipped (matching pre-PR behavior); `memory/` and `MEMORY.md` continue to follow symlinks.
- Idempotence guard expanded to `this.watcher || this.nativeMemoryWatchers.length > 0` so a re-entrant `ensureWatcher()` doesn't double-allocate native watchers.

`extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts` (+10/-0):
- `close()` tears down the native watchers in addition to the chokidar watcher.

`extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.watcher-config.test.ts` (+230/-67):
- Adds a `nativeWatchMock` paired with the new factory key.
- Existing assertions split: chokidar mock receives only file paths (`MEMORY.md`); native mock receives directories (`memory/`, dir-typed `extraPaths`) with `recursive: true`.
- New test cases:
  - Native `rename` / `change` event → `markDirty`.
  - Null filename event → broad `markDirty` (Node platform caveat).
  - Native creation failure → fallback into chokidar's path set, with the warning logged.
  - Native runtime error → watcher closed, removed, broad `markDirty` fires.
  - `ensureWatcher()` re-entrant call → no duplicate native watcher allocation.

## Compatibility expectations

- **Memory freshness semantics preserved.** Every path that previously triggered `markDirty` continues to trigger it. The shared `shouldIgnoreMemoryWatchPath` predicate is the sole filter for both watcher backends.
- **No config knobs added or removed.** `sync.watch` resolution at `src/agents/memory-search.ts` is unchanged; this is purely an internal watcher backend swap. The "watcher policy choice" raised in clawsweeper's review on #86613 (disable / cap / lifetime change) is intentionally sidestepped — this PR is a transparent infrastructure swap.
- **No new dependencies.** Uses only `node:fs` recursive watch, available in our `engines: node >= 22.19`.
- **Cross-platform.** Recursive `fs.watch` is supported in Node ≥22.19 on macOS (FSEvents), Linux (since v19.1, refined through v20+), and Windows (always supported). On any path where the native call throws or the FSWatcher emits an error, the chokidar fallback preserves coverage. Real lab verification was done on macOS only (see "What I did not test" above).

## Acceptance criteria from clawsweeper review on #86613

- [x] Real-chokidar large-tree `/tools/invoke memory_search` repro that samples gateway FD counts before and after the call — done in lab (Environment 2 above)
- [x] `manager.watcher-config.test.ts` updated for the watcher split + new behavior cases
- [x] `tools.test.ts` runs clean against the patch (untouched file; verified locally)
- [x] `tools-invoke-http.test.ts` runs clean against the patch (untouched file; verified locally)

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm tsgo:core:test` clean on the patched source
- [ ] `pnpm test:extension memory-core` clean
- [ ] `pnpm check` clean
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.watcher-config.test.ts` clean
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts` clean
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.test.ts` clean
- [x] Local `codex review --base origin/main` run; findings addressed (see "AI assistance" below)
- [x] Lab probe against patched dist: `12,392 → 1` REG FD reduction confirmed, second-call returns `200 OK` with zero FD delta (numbers above)

## AI assistance

- [x] AI-assisted: drafted with Claude (Sonnet 4.6 / Opus 4.7). Patch design, test scaffolding, and PR body all went through structured codex peer review.
- [x] Human-run real behavior proof included (lab + deployed-gateway numbers above; manual `curl POST /tools/invoke` and `lsof` sampling done by hand on a real Mac).
- [x] Codex review session log available on request.
- [x] I understand what the code does and the trade-offs (see "Compatibility expectations" + "What I did not test").
- [x] Local `codex review --base origin/main` run before opening.
- [x] Will resolve any bot review conversations rather than leaving them for maintainers.

## Notes for review

- This is a regression fix. The previous behavior (chokidar's per-file fs.watch fan-out) was opportunistically introduced when the watch target changed from glob to bare directory in #64711; this PR restores the small-constant-FD-cost property the gateway had before that change, without reverting the directory-watch semantics.
- The PR scope is intentionally narrow: only the watcher backend selection changes. No changes to `sync.watch` default, no per-tree cap, no manager lifetime change. The "maintainer policy direction" called out by clawsweeper is sidestepped because freshness semantics are preserved.
- Reviewers may want to consider whether `memorySearch.sync.watch: false` should still be the safer default for very large workspaces as defense-in-depth (separately from this PR). That's #71335's domain.

## Codex review findings deferred to follow-up

1. **`close()` / sync race** (pre-existing). `codex review --base origin/main` flagged a P2 race in `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts` around `close()` lines 940–941: when `close()` races with a first `sync()` that's still awaiting `ensureProviderInitialized()`, the `pendingSync` snapshot taken before the await can be stale (`this.syncing` may not yet be assigned). After provider init resolves, that sync continues and can run against the just-closed provider/DB. This race is in pre-existing code; my changes in this file are limited to the native watcher teardown block at lines 958–967 (a disjoint region). Tracked as separate follow-up in **#86702**.

2. **Watched directory replacement** (edge case, surfaced by post-fallback-fix codex review). When an existing `memory/` directory is deleted and recreated mid-process (e.g. `rm -rf memory && mkdir memory`), native `fs.watch` remains bound to the old inode and subsequent changes in the recreated directory are not observed. With `intervalMinutes` defaulting to `0`, auto-sync would stay stale until restart. The previous chokidar-on-bare-directory setup may handle this case via parent-dir or rediscovery semantics (not fully verified). Fixing this here would require either parent-directory watching, periodic inode-stat checks, or readdirp-style root rediscovery — meaningful scope expansion beyond the FD-leak fix. The scenario is uncommon at runtime (live gateways rarely have their memory roots replaced) and is also relevant to #71335 (sync.watch policy: the safer default for high-churn workspaces is `sync.watch: false` plus a non-zero `intervalMinutes`). Happy to file as a separate follow-up issue if maintainers consider it merge-blocking.

## Update — clawsweeper review addressed

clawsweeper's PR review (2026-05-26 01:32 UTC) requested one fix before merge: the native watcher `error` handler closed and removed the failed watcher but did not restore coverage, leaving the directory un-watched. This is now fixed in the follow-up commit on this branch:

- `manager-sync-ops.ts` — added `attachMemoryChokidarFallback(dir, markDirty)`. On a native watcher runtime error, the directory is reattached to the existing chokidar watcher via `this.watcher.add(dir)`; if no chokidar watcher exists yet, one is spun up for that directory.
- `manager.watcher-config.test.ts` — the existing runtime-error test now asserts the directory is added to chokidar after the error, AND that a subsequent chokidar event on the fallback path schedules `sync`. New test added for the "no chokidar yet → spin one up" branch.

All 16 tests in `manager.watcher-config.test.ts` pass; lab probe still shows 12,392 → 1 REG FD on the synthetic workspace.

Closes #86613.

target.title: discord/picker: alpha-bucket select for >25 providers or models
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- `provider/*` wildcard configs frequently expand to 50+ models for a single provider; the Discord model picker rendered them as long prev/next paginated dropdowns that were tedious to scan.

Why does this matter now?

- The wildcard rollout is making large-provider lists the common case (vLLM auto-discovery in particular), and the existing picker UX assumed sub-25-item lists.

What is the intended outcome?

- Provider selection always renders as a dropdown with pagination instead of provider buttons. When a provider or model list exceeds Discord's 25-option select cap, a letter-range bucket select appears above the relevant dropdown (`A–G (12) · H–N (18) · O–Z (5)`).

What is intentionally out of scope?

- The wildcard-load defer fix (event-loop blocking from unbounded catalog load) ships as a separate PR — it is independent of this UX change and reviewable on its own merits.
- Telegram / Slack / Mattermost / WhatsApp pickers — Telegram has the same strict-equality landmines on a different code path; Slack and Mattermost have no picker yet. Each warrants its own change.

What does success look like?

- Users with 100+ models for one provider can jump straight to the right letter range with one click instead of paging through 4–5 prev/next clicks.
- No regression for users whose configs stay under 25 items.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Discord component limits: providers now render as one string-select row with pagination, not a provider button grid, so the picker stays under both the 5-row and 25-option limits (`model-picker.state.ts`, `model-picker.view.ts`).
- customId encoding: bucket state is derived at re-render via `findProviderBucketId` / `findModelBucketId` where possible, while provider select and nav customIds carry only the state needed to preserve the active page/bucket.

## Linked context

Closes #

Related #

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- No — author-initiated UX improvement.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Discord `/models` and `/model` pickers need to stay usable when wildcard providers expose large provider/model catalogs. Provider selection now stays in a dropdown with pagination, and model/provider bucket selectors keep the visible options under Discord's 25-option select cap.
- Real environment tested: local vitest against the PR worktree plus a rebuilt Docker dev gateway running the Discord plugin with the Dev bot in a real Discord guild.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=30000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-discord.config.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/model-picker.test.ts`
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=30000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-discord.config.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/native-command.model-picker.test.ts`
  - `docker compose build openclaw-gateway`
  - `docker compose up -d --force-recreate openclaw-gateway`
  - `curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:19250/healthz`
  - Manual Discord `/model` invocation against the dev bot.
- Evidence after fix:
  - `model-picker.test.ts`: `Test Files 1 passed (1) / Tests 39 passed (39)`
  - `native-command.model-picker.test.ts`: `Test Files 1 passed (1) / Tests 18 passed (18)`
  - Docker dev gateway health: `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}`
  - Gateway logs show the Discord plugin loaded and the bot initialized as `@DevMan` in guild `1478433853671739434`.
  - Live Discord screenshot: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rendrag-git/openclaw/pr-assets-86181/pr-assets/86181/live-discord-model-picker.png
- Observed result after fix: the live Discord picker renders the bucket dropdown in the model picker, and the provider view renders providers through a dropdown instead of provider buttons while preserving pagination/bucket state.
- What was not tested: automated CI cannot exercise the live Discord guild/bot. The live Discord proof is manual dev-gateway smoke evidence; the serialized Discord component payloads and interaction handler paths are covered by focused tests.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the dev gateway is a Docker-local environment on port 19250 using the configured Discord Dev bot token; it is not a packaged release install.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the original branch used provider buttons and had a model-bucketing path where the provider selector disappeared with no route back to provider selection.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/discord/src/monitor/model-picker.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/native-command.model-picker.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/model-picker-preferences.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs`
- `/home/ubuntu/projects/codexcustomization/reviewloop/bin/reviewloop -- --base main` (3 passes; findings from each pass closed and pinned with regression tests)

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- `computeAlphaBuckets`: single-bucket below threshold, letter ranges for diverse input, no stray-letter spillover, numeric fallback when prefixes collide, empty input, bucket count ≤ 25 for 501- and 5000-item lists.
- `getDiscordModelPickerProviderPage` / `getDiscordModelPickerModelPage`: bucket-aware paging, single-page fits within bucketed row budget.
- Provider view renders a provider select rather than provider buttons; provider select and nav rows preserve active bucket/page state.
- Model select customId asserts length ≤ 100 chars for long provider + snowflake user id with full bucket state.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- N/A — this is the introducing PR. Earlier branch commits pinned three reviewloop-surfaced regressions (customId overflow, row-budget overrun, model-pagination state drop) that are now closed.

If no test was added, why not?

- All findings have regression coverage.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`)

- Yes — Discord `/models` and `/model` pickers use dropdown-based provider selection, with letter-range bucket selects when provider or model count exceeds 25.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?

- customId state preservation across the picker's interaction graph. Provider/model buckets and pages must survive dropdown selection, pagination, and the Providers back path.

How is that risk mitigated?

- Regression tests pin provider-select rendering, bucket/page preservation, and customId length ≤ 100. Manual round-trip testing against the live Discord dev bot now verifies the bucket picker appears in a real guild.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Maintainer review of the dropdown provider selection, bucket-state derivation contract (`findProviderBucketId` / `findModelBucketId`), and live Discord proof.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- CI and maintainer review. Live Discord interaction proof is now attached as a screenshot from the dev gateway.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Reviewloop pass 1: dispatch/persist double-write race in apply path — addressed by a separate PR (out of scope here).
- Reviewloop pass 2: bucket count > 25 for huge same-prefix lists; modelBucket round-trip on model select — addressed in 4fdeca0.
- Reviewloop pass 3: customId length > 100 for long provider + 20-digit user id; row budget overrun with bucket select active; providerBucket dropped on model pagination — addressed in a4960776.
